## Exenciones de Bancarrota en Nueva York [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcyexemptionsbystate.com/states-es/new-york.html

Exenciones por Estado | Todos los Estados | English | Calculadora | Screener

Exenciones de Bancarrota en Nueva York

Guia completa: exenciones, limites de la prueba de medios, costos de presentacion y datos reales de casos en Nueva York.

Nota importante para inmigrantes: La bancarrota NO afecta su estatus migratorio. Residentes permanentes, titulares de visa y personas indocumentadas pueden declararse en bancarrota. Es un derecho federal disponible para todos los residentes de Estados Unidos.

Importante: Esta pagina proporciona informacion educativa sobre las exenciones de bancarrota en Nueva York. No constituye asesoramiento legal. Las cifras de ingresos medianos son aproximadas (Censo/DOJ, vigentes abril 2026). Verifique los montos actuales en la pagina del DOJ.

4

Distritos de Bancarrota

$72,050

Mediana (1 Persona)

$111,130

Mediana (Familia de 4)

$338

Cuota Cap. 7

Exenciones de Nueva York

Solo Exenciones Estatales

Nueva York requiere que los declarantes utilicen solo las exenciones estatales. Las exenciones federales bajo 11 U.S.C. Seccion 522(d) no estan disponibles.

Exencion de Hogar (Homestead)

Protege el patrimonio de su residencia principal. El monto varia segun los estatutos de Nueva York. Consulte las leyes locales para limites actuales.

Exencion de Vehiculo

Protege el patrimonio en su vehiculo hasta un limite establecido por ley estatal. Verifique los montos actuales de Nueva York.

Propiedad Personal

Incluye muebles, ropa, electrodomesticos y otros articulos personales hasta los limites estatales.

Exencion Comodin (Wildcard)

Consulte los estatutos de Nueva York para disponibilidad y montos. Le permite proteger cualquier propiedad hasta un monto determinado.

Prueba de Medios (Means Test) en Nueva York

Tamano del HogarIngreso Mediano AnualIngreso Mediano Mensual

1 persona$72,050$6,004

2 personas$91,504$7,625

3 personas$109,516$9,126

4 personas$111,130$9,261

Por cada persona adicional arriba de 4, agregue aproximadamente $11,100. Cifras vigentes abril 2026. Fuente: Censo/DOJ.

Costos de Presentacion

Cuotas del Tribunal

Capitulo 7: $338. Capitulo 13: $313. Se pueden solicitar exenciones de cuotas si sus ingresos estan por debajo del 150% de la linea de pobreza federal.

Honorarios de Abogado

Capitulo 7: $1,200 - $3,500. Capitulo 13: $3,000 - $5,500. Puede presentar sin abogado (pro se).

Preguntas Frecuentes

Cuales son las exenciones de bancarrota en Nueva York?

Las principales exenciones incluyen: exencion de hogar (homestead), exencion de vehiculo, propiedad personal, cuentas de jubilacion y exencion comodin. Los declarantes deben usar las exenciones estatales de Nueva York. Consulte los estatutos locales para montos actuales.

Cual es el limite de ingresos para el Capitulo 7 en Nueva York?

La prueba de medios compara sus ingresos con la mediana de Nueva York. Para una persona sola, el umbral es aproximadamente $72,050 al anno. Para una familia de cuatro, aproximadamente $111,130. Si sus ingresos estan por debajo de la mediana, pasa la Parte 1 de la prueba.

La bancarrota afecta mi estatus migratorio?

No. Declararse en bancarrota NO afecta su estatus migratorio. Es un derecho federal. Residentes permanentes, titulares de visa y personas indocumentadas pueden presentar una solicitud.

Puedo conservar mi casa en una bancarrota en Nueva York?

Depende de su patrimonio (equity) y la exencion de hogar de Nueva York. Si su patrimonio esta dentro del limite de exencion, generalmente puede conservar su casa en el Capitulo 7. En el Capitulo 13, puede conservarla mientras realiza los pagos del plan.

## Exenciones de Bancarrota en Oregón [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcyexemptionsbystate.com/states-es/oregon.html

Exenciones por Estado | Todos los Estados | English | Calculadora | Screener

Exenciones de Bancarrota en Oregón

Guia completa: exenciones, limites de la prueba de medios, costos de presentacion y datos reales de casos en Oregón.

Nota importante para inmigrantes: La bancarrota NO afecta su estatus migratorio. Residentes permanentes, titulares de visa y personas indocumentadas pueden declararse en bancarrota. Es un derecho federal disponible para todos los residentes de Estados Unidos.

Importante: Esta pagina proporciona informacion educativa sobre las exenciones de bancarrota en Oregón. No constituye asesoramiento legal. Las cifras de ingresos medianos son aproximadas (Censo/DOJ, vigentes abril 2026). Verifique los montos actuales en la pagina del DOJ.

1

Distritos de Bancarrota

$66,040

Mediana (1 Persona)

$104,420

Mediana (Familia de 4)

$338

Cuota Cap. 7

Exenciones de Oregón

Exenciones Federales Disponibles

Oregón permite a los declarantes elegir entre las exenciones estatales y las exenciones federales bajo 11 U.S.C. Seccion 522(d).

Exencion de Hogar (Homestead)

Protege el patrimonio de su residencia principal. El monto varia segun los estatutos de Oregón. Consulte las leyes locales para limites actuales.

Exencion de Vehiculo

Protege el patrimonio en su vehiculo hasta un limite establecido por ley estatal. Verifique los montos actuales de Oregón.

Propiedad Personal

Incluye muebles, ropa, electrodomesticos y otros articulos personales hasta los limites estatales.

Exencion Comodin (Wildcard)

Disponible bajo exenciones federales o estatales. Le permite proteger cualquier propiedad hasta un monto determinado.

Prueba de Medios (Means Test) en Oregón

Tamano del HogarIngreso Mediano AnualIngreso Mediano Mensual

1 persona$66,040$5,503

2 personas$83,871$6,989

3 personas$100,381$8,365

4 personas$104,420$8,702

Por cada persona adicional arriba de 4, agregue aproximadamente $11,100. Cifras vigentes abril 2026. Fuente: Censo/DOJ.

Costos de Presentacion

Cuotas del Tribunal

Capitulo 7: $338. Capitulo 13: $313. Se pueden solicitar exenciones de cuotas si sus ingresos estan por debajo del 150% de la linea de pobreza federal.

Honorarios de Abogado

Capitulo 7: $1,200 - $3,500. Capitulo 13: $3,000 - $5,500. Puede presentar sin abogado (pro se).

Preguntas Frecuentes

Cuales son las exenciones de bancarrota en Oregón?

Las principales exenciones incluyen: exencion de hogar (homestead), exencion de vehiculo, propiedad personal, cuentas de jubilacion y exencion comodin. Los declarantes pueden elegir entre exenciones estatales o federales. Consulte los estatutos locales para montos actuales.

Cual es el limite de ingresos para el Capitulo 7 en Oregón?

La prueba de medios compara sus ingresos con la mediana de Oregón. Para una persona sola, el umbral es aproximadamente $66,040 al anno. Para una familia de cuatro, aproximadamente $104,420. Si sus ingresos estan por debajo de la mediana, pasa la Parte 1 de la prueba.

La bancarrota afecta mi estatus migratorio?

No. Declararse en bancarrota NO afecta su estatus migratorio. Es un derecho federal. Residentes permanentes, titulares de visa y personas indocumentadas pueden presentar una solicitud.

Puedo conservar mi casa en una bancarrota en Oregón?

Depende de su patrimonio (equity) y la exencion de hogar de Oregón. Si su patrimonio esta dentro del limite de exencion, generalmente puede conservar su casa en el Capitulo 7. En el Capitulo 13, puede conservarla mientras realiza los pagos del plan.

Exenciones en Estados Cercanos

Washington · California · Idaho · Nevada

Herramientas Gratuitas de Bancarrota

## Exenciones de Bancarrota en Pensilvania [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcyexemptionsbystate.com/states-es/pennsylvania.html

Exenciones por Estado | Todos los Estados | English | Calculadora | Screener

Exenciones de Bancarrota en Pensilvania

Guia completa: exenciones, limites de la prueba de medios, costos de presentacion y datos reales de casos en Pensilvania.

Nota importante para inmigrantes: La bancarrota NO afecta su estatus migratorio. Residentes permanentes, titulares de visa y personas indocumentadas pueden declararse en bancarrota. Es un derecho federal disponible para todos los residentes de Estados Unidos.

Importante: Esta pagina proporciona informacion educativa sobre las exenciones de bancarrota en Pensilvania. No constituye asesoramiento legal. Las cifras de ingresos medianos son aproximadas (Censo/DOJ, vigentes abril 2026). Verifique los montos actuales en la pagina del DOJ.

3

Distritos de Bancarrota

$64,470

Mediana (1 Persona)

$105,720

Mediana (Familia de 4)

$338

Cuota Cap. 7

Exenciones de Pensilvania

Exenciones Federales Disponibles

Pensilvania permite a los declarantes elegir entre las exenciones estatales y las exenciones federales bajo 11 U.S.C. Seccion 522(d).

Exencion de Hogar (Homestead)

Protege el patrimonio de su residencia principal. El monto varia segun los estatutos de Pensilvania. Consulte las leyes locales para limites actuales.

Exencion de Vehiculo

Protege el patrimonio en su vehiculo hasta un limite establecido por ley estatal. Verifique los montos actuales de Pensilvania.

Propiedad Personal

Incluye muebles, ropa, electrodomesticos y otros articulos personales hasta los limites estatales.

Exencion Comodin (Wildcard)

Disponible bajo exenciones federales o estatales. Le permite proteger cualquier propiedad hasta un monto determinado.

Prueba de Medios (Means Test) en Pensilvania

Tamano del HogarIngreso Mediano AnualIngreso Mediano Mensual

1 persona$64,470$5,372

2 personas$81,877$6,823

3 personas$97,994$8,166

4 personas$105,720$8,810

Por cada persona adicional arriba de 4, agregue aproximadamente $11,100. Cifras vigentes abril 2026. Fuente: Censo/DOJ.

Costos de Presentacion

Cuotas del Tribunal

Capitulo 7: $338. Capitulo 13: $313. Se pueden solicitar exenciones de cuotas si sus ingresos estan por debajo del 150% de la linea de pobreza federal.

Honorarios de Abogado

Capitulo 7: $1,000 - $3,000. Capitulo 13: $2,500 - $5,000. Puede presentar sin abogado (pro se).

Preguntas Frecuentes

Cuales son las exenciones de bancarrota en Pensilvania?

Las principales exenciones incluyen: exencion de hogar (homestead), exencion de vehiculo, propiedad personal, cuentas de jubilacion y exencion comodin. Los declarantes pueden elegir entre exenciones estatales o federales. Consulte los estatutos locales para montos actuales.

Cual es el limite de ingresos para el Capitulo 7 en Pensilvania?

La prueba de medios compara sus ingresos con la mediana de Pensilvania. Para una persona sola, el umbral es aproximadamente $64,470 al anno. Para una familia de cuatro, aproximadamente $105,720. Si sus ingresos estan por debajo de la mediana, pasa la Parte 1 de la prueba.

La bancarrota afecta mi estatus migratorio?

No. Declararse en bancarrota NO afecta su estatus migratorio. Es un derecho federal. Residentes permanentes, titulares de visa y personas indocumentadas pueden presentar una solicitud.

Puedo conservar mi casa en una bancarrota en Pensilvania?

Depende de su patrimonio (equity) y la exencion de hogar de Pensilvania. Si su patrimonio esta dentro del limite de exencion, generalmente puede conservar su casa en el Capitulo 7. En el Capitulo 13, puede conservarla mientras realiza los pagos del plan.

Exencion de Hogar (Homestead)

Protege el patrimonio de su residencia principal. El monto varia segun los estatutos de Rhode Island. Consulte las leyes locales para limites actuales.

Exencion de Vehiculo

Protege el patrimonio en su vehiculo hasta un limite establecido por ley estatal. Verifique los montos actuales de Rhode Island.

Propiedad Personal

Incluye muebles, ropa, electrodomesticos y otros articulos personales hasta los limites estatales.

Solo Exenciones Estatales

Texas permite a los declarantes elegir entre las exenciones estatales y las exenciones federales bajo 11 U.S.C. Seccion 522(d).

Exencion de Hogar (Homestead)

Protege el patrimonio de su residencia principal. El monto varia segun los estatutos de Texas. Consulte las leyes locales para limites actuales.

Exencion de Vehiculo

Protege el patrimonio en su vehiculo hasta un limite establecido por ley estatal. Verifique los montos actuales de Texas.

Propiedad Personal

Incluye muebles, ropa, electrodomesticos y otros articulos personales hasta los limites estatales.

Exencion Comodin (Wildcard)

Disponible bajo exenciones federales o estatales. Le permite proteger cualquier propiedad hasta un monto determinado.

Prueba de Medios (Means Test) en Texas

Tamano del HogarIngreso Mediano AnualIngreso Mediano Mensual

1 persona$64,260$5,355

2 personas$81,610$6,801

3 personas$97,675$8,140

4 personas$101,440$8,453

Por cada persona adicional arriba de 4, agregue aproximadamente $11,100. Cifras vigentes abril 2026. Fuente: Censo/DOJ.

Costos de Presentacion

Cuotas del Tribunal

Capitulo 7: $338. Capitulo 13: $313. Se pueden solicitar exenciones de cuotas si sus ingresos estan por debajo del 150% de la linea de pobreza federal.

Honorarios de Abogado

Capitulo 7: $1,000 - $3,000. Capitulo 13: $2,500 - $5,000. Puede presentar sin abogado (pro se).

Preguntas Frecuentes

Cuales son las exenciones de bancarrota en Texas?

Las principales exenciones incluyen: exencion de hogar (homestead), exencion de vehiculo, propiedad personal, cuentas de jubilacion y exencion comodin. Los declarantes pueden elegir entre exenciones estatales o federales. Consulte los estatutos locales para montos actuales.

Cual es el limite de ingresos para el Capitulo 7 en Texas?

La prueba de medios compara sus ingresos con la mediana de Texas. Para una persona sola, el umbral es aproximadamente $64,260 al anno. Para una familia de cuatro, aproximadamente $101,440. Si sus ingresos estan por debajo de la mediana, pasa la Parte 1 de la prueba.

La bancarrota afecta mi estatus migratorio?

No. Declararse en bancarrota NO afecta su estatus migratorio. Es un derecho federal. Residentes permanentes, titulares de visa y personas indocumentadas pueden presentar una solicitud.

Puedo conservar mi casa en una bancarrota en Texas?

Depende de su patrimonio (equity) y la exencion de hogar de Texas. Si su patrimonio esta dentro del limite de exencion, generalmente puede conservar su casa en el Capitulo 7. En el Capitulo 13, puede conservarla mientras realiza los pagos del plan.

Exenciones en Estados Cercanos

Luisiana · Arkansas · Oklahoma · Nuevo México

Herramientas Gratuitas de Bancarrota

Exenciones de Bancarrota en Utah

Guia completa: exenciones, limites de la prueba de medios, costos de presentacion y datos reales de casos en Utah.

Nota importante para inmigrantes: La bancarrota NO afecta su estatus migratorio. Residentes permanentes, titulares de visa y personas indocumentadas pueden declararse en bancarrota. Es un derecho federal disponible para todos los residentes de Estados Unidos.

Exenciones por Estado | Todos los Estados | English | Calculadora | Screener

Exenciones de Bancarrota en Virginia Occidental

Guia completa: exenciones, limites de la prueba de medios, costos de presentacion y datos reales de casos en Virginia Occidental.

Nota importante para inmigrantes: La bancarrota NO afecta su estatus migratorio. Residentes permanentes, titulares de visa y personas indocumentadas pueden declararse en bancarrota. Es un derecho federal disponible para todos los residentes de Estados Unidos.

Importante: Esta pagina proporciona informacion educativa sobre las exenciones de bancarrota en Virginia Occidental. No constituye asesoramiento legal. Las cifras de ingresos medianos son aproximadas (Censo/DOJ, vigentes abril 2026). Verifique los montos actuales en la pagina del DOJ.

Distritos de Bancarrota

$48,840

Mediana (1 Persona)

$85,060

Mediana (Familia de 4)

$338

Cuota Cap. 7

Exenciones de Virginia Occidental

Solo Exenciones Estatales

Virginia Occidental requiere que los declarantes utilicen solo las exenciones estatales. Las exenciones federales bajo 11 U.S.C. Seccion 522(d) no estan disponibles.

Exencion de Hogar (Homestead)

Protege el patrimonio de su residencia principal. El monto varia segun los estatutos de Virginia Occidental. Consulte las leyes locales para limites actuales.

Exencion de Vehiculo

Protege el patrimonio en su vehiculo hasta un limite establecido por ley estatal. Verifique los montos actuales de Virginia Occidental.

Propiedad Personal

Incluye muebles, ropa, electrodomesticos y otros articulos personales hasta los limites estatales.

Exencion Comodin (Wildcard)

Consulte los estatutos de Virginia Occidental para disponibilidad y montos. Le permite proteger cualquier propiedad hasta un monto determinado.

Prueba de Medios (Means Test) en Virginia Occidental

Tamano del HogarIngreso Mediano AnualIngreso Mediano Mensual

1 persona$48,840$4,070

2 personas$62,027$5,169

3 personas$74,237$6,186

4 personas$85,060$7,088

Por cada persona adicional arriba de 4, agregue aproximadamente $11,100. Cifras vigentes abril 2026. Fuente: Censo/DOJ.

Costos de Presentacion

Cuotas del Tribunal

Capitulo 7: $338. Capitulo 13: $313. Se pueden solicitar exenciones de cuotas si sus ingresos estan por debajo del 150% de la linea de pobreza federal.

Honorarios de Abogado

Capitulo 7: $800 - $2,500. Capitulo 13: $2,000 - $4,500. Puede presentar sin abogado (pro se).

Preguntas Frecuentes

Cuales son las exenciones de bancarrota en Virginia Occidental?

Las principales exenciones incluyen: exencion de hogar (homestead), exencion de vehiculo, propiedad personal, cuentas de jubilacion y exencion comodin. Los declarantes deben usar las exenciones estatales de Virginia Occidental. Consulte los estatutos locales para montos actuales.

Cual es el limite de ingresos para el Capitulo 7 en Virginia Occidental?

La prueba de medios compara sus ingresos con la mediana de Virginia Occidental. Para una persona sola, el umbral es aproximadamente $48,840 al anno. Para una familia de cuatro, aproximadamente $85,060. Si sus ingresos estan por debajo de la mediana, pasa la Parte 1 de la prueba.

La bancarrota afecta mi estatus migratorio?

No. Declararse en bancarrota NO afecta su estatus migratorio. Es un derecho federal. Residentes permanentes, titulares de visa y personas indocumentadas pueden presentar una solicitud.

Puedo conservar mi casa en una bancarrota en Virginia Occidental?

Depende de su patrimonio (equity) y la exencion de hogar de Virginia Occidental. Si su patrimonio esta dentro del limite de exencion, generalmente puede conservar su casa en el Capitulo 7. En el Capitulo 13, puede conservarla mientras realiza los pagos del plan.

$104,950

Mediana (Familia de 4)

$338

Cuota Cap. 7

Exenciones de Wisconsin

Exenciones Federales Disponibles

Wisconsin permite a los declarantes elegir entre las exenciones estatales y las exenciones federales bajo 11 U.S.C. Seccion 522(d).

Exencion de Hogar (Homestead)

Protege el patrimonio de su residencia principal. El monto varia segun los estatutos de Wisconsin. Consulte las leyes locales para limites actuales.

Exencion de Vehiculo

Protege el patrimonio en su vehiculo hasta un limite establecido por ley estatal. Verifique los montos actuales de Wisconsin.

Propiedad Personal

Incluye muebles, ropa, electrodomesticos y otros articulos personales hasta los limites estatales.

Exencion Comodin (Wildcard)

Disponible bajo exenciones federales o estatales. Le permite proteger cualquier propiedad hasta un monto determinado.

Prueba de Medios (Means Test) en Wisconsin

Tamano del HogarIngreso Mediano AnualIngreso Mediano Mensual

1 persona$65,080$5,423

2 personas$82,652$6,888

3 personas$98,922$8,243

4 personas$104,950$8,746

Por cada persona adicional arriba de 4, agregue aproximadamente $11,100. Cifras vigentes abril 2026. Fuente: Censo/DOJ.

Costos de Presentacion

Cuotas del Tribunal

Capitulo 7: $338. Capitulo 13: $313. Se pueden solicitar exenciones de cuotas si sus ingresos estan por debajo del 150% de la linea de pobreza federal.

Honorarios de Abogado

Capitulo 7: $1,200 - $3,500. Capitulo 13: $3,000 - $5,500. Puede presentar sin abogado (pro se).

Preguntas Frecuentes

Cuales son las exenciones de bancarrota en Wisconsin?

Las principales exenciones incluyen: exencion de hogar (homestead), exencion de vehiculo, propiedad personal, cuentas de jubilacion y exencion comodin. Los declarantes pueden elegir entre exenciones estatales o federales. Consulte los estatutos locales para montos actuales.

Cual es el limite de ingresos para el Capitulo 7 en Wisconsin?

La prueba de medios compara sus ingresos con la mediana de Wisconsin. Para una persona sola, el umbral es aproximadamente $65,080 al anno. Para una familia de cuatro, aproximadamente $104,950. Si sus ingresos estan por debajo de la mediana, pasa la Parte 1 de la prueba.

La bancarrota afecta mi estatus migratorio?

No. Declararse en bancarrota NO afecta su estatus migratorio. Es un derecho federal. Residentes permanentes, titulares de visa y personas indocumentadas pueden presentar una solicitud.

Puedo conservar mi casa en una bancarrota en Wisconsin?

Depende de su patrimonio (equity) y la exencion de hogar de Wisconsin. Si su patrimonio esta dentro del limite de exencion, generalmente puede conservar su casa en el Capitulo 7. En el Capitulo 13, puede conservarla mientras realiza los pagos del plan.

Exenciones en Estados Cercanos

Minnesota · Míchigan · Illinois · Iowa

Herramientas Gratuitas de Bancarrota

Del Open Bankruptcy Project -- informacion gratuita, sin compromisos.

Screener de Descarga
Calculadora de Medios

Parte del Open Bankruptcy Project | Informacion gratuita, sin compromisos.

Suspension Automatica ·
Reunion 341 ·
Exenciones por Estado ·
Guia de Costos ·
Cap 7 vs 13 ·
Como Declararse ·
Deudas No Descargables

## Exenciones de Bancarrota en Wyoming [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcyexemptionsbystate.com/states-es/wyoming.html

Exenciones por Estado | Todos los Estados | English | Calculadora | Screener

Exenciones de Bancarrota en Wyoming

Guia completa: exenciones, limites de la prueba de medios, costos de presentacion y datos reales de casos en Wyoming.

Nota importante para inmigrantes: La bancarrota NO afecta su estatus migratorio. Residentes permanentes, titulares de visa y personas indocumentadas pueden declararse en bancarrota. Es un derecho federal disponible para todos los residentes de Estados Unidos.

Importante: Esta pagina proporciona informacion educativa sobre las exenciones de bancarrota en Wyoming. No constituye asesoramiento legal. Las cifras de ingresos medianos son aproximadas (Censo/DOJ, vigentes abril 2026). Verifique los montos actuales en la pagina del DOJ.

1

Distritos de Bancarrota

$67,360

Mediana (1 Persona)

$107,280

Mediana (Familia de 4)

$338

Cuota Cap. 7

Exenciones de Wyoming

Solo Exenciones Estatales

Wyoming requiere que los declarantes utilicen solo las exenciones estatales. Las exenciones federales bajo 11 U.S.C. Seccion 522(d) no estan disponibles.

Exencion de Hogar (Homestead)

Protege el patrimonio de su residencia principal. El monto varia segun los estatutos de Wyoming. Consulte las leyes locales para limites actuales.

Exencion de Vehiculo

Protege el patrimonio en su vehiculo hasta un limite establecido por ley estatal. Verifique los montos actuales de Wyoming.

Propiedad Personal

Incluye muebles, ropa, electrodomesticos y otros articulos personales hasta los limites estatales.

Exencion Comodin (Wildcard)

Consulte los estatutos de Wyoming para disponibilidad y montos. Le permite proteger cualquier propiedad hasta un monto determinado.

Prueba de Medios (Means Test) en Wyoming

Tamano del HogarIngreso Mediano AnualIngreso Mediano Mensual

1 persona$67,360$5,613

2 personas$85,547$7,129

3 personas$102,387$8,532

4 personas$107,280$8,940

Por cada persona adicional arriba de 4, agregue aproximadamente $11,100. Cifras vigentes abril 2026. Fuente: Censo/DOJ.

Costos de Presentacion

Cuotas del Tribunal

Capitulo 7: $338. Capitulo 13: $313. Se pueden solicitar exenciones de cuotas si sus ingresos estan por debajo del 150% de la linea de pobreza federal.

Honorarios de Abogado

Capitulo 7: $1,200 - $3,500. Capitulo 13: $3,000 - $5,500. Puede presentar sin abogado (pro se).

Preguntas Frecuentes

Cuales son las exenciones de bancarrota en Wyoming?

Las principales exenciones incluyen: exencion de hogar (homestead), exencion de vehiculo, propiedad personal, cuentas de jubilacion y exencion comodin. Los declarantes deben usar las exenciones estatales de Wyoming. Consulte los estatutos locales para montos actuales.

Cual es el limite de ingresos para el Capitulo 7 en Wyoming?

La prueba de medios compara sus ingresos con la mediana de Wyoming. Para una persona sola, el umbral es aproximadamente $67,360 al anno. Para una familia de cuatro, aproximadamente $107,280. Si sus ingresos estan por debajo de la mediana, pasa la Parte 1 de la prueba.

La bancarrota afecta mi estatus migratorio?

No. Declararse en bancarrota NO afecta su estatus migratorio. Es un derecho federal. Residentes permanentes, titulares de visa y personas indocumentadas pueden presentar una solicitud.

Puedo conservar mi casa en una bancarrota en Wyoming?

Depende de su patrimonio (equity) y la exencion de hogar de Wyoming. Si su patrimonio esta dentro del limite de exencion, generalmente puede conservar su casa en el Capitulo 7. En el Capitulo 13, puede conservarla mientras realiza los pagos del plan.

Exenciones en Estados Cercanos

Montana · Dakota del Sur · Nebraska · Colorado · Utah · Idaho

## Bankruptcy in Alabama: Filing Guide, Exemptions, and Statistics [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcyexemptionsbystate.com/states/alabama.html

Exemptions by State | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Bankruptcy in Alabama

Complete guide: exemptions, means test income limits, filing costs, and real case outcome data for Alabama.

Important: This page provides educational information about bankruptcy in Alabama. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

3

Bankruptcy Districts

$56,050

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Alabama Exemptions

State Exemptions Only

Alabama requires bankruptcy filers to use state exemptions exclusively. Federal exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available. Research Alabama's homestead, vehicle, personal property, and wildcard exemptions before filing.

Homestead Exemption

Protects equity in your primary residence. Amounts vary by state. Check Alabama homestead exemption amounts.

Vehicle Exemption

Protects equity in your car. Most states protect $2,000 to $7,500. See vehicle exemption details.

Retirement Accounts

401(k)s, IRAs, and pensions are generally fully exempt in bankruptcy. See retirement exemptions.

Wildcard Exemption

Applies to any property. Not all states offer this. See wildcard exemptions.

Alabama Means Test Income Limits

If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 of the means test and likely qualify for Chapter 7.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$56,050$4,671

2 persons$72,865$6,072

3 persons$85,196$7,100

4 persons$99,208$8,267

5 persons$110,308$9,192

6 persons$121,408$10,117

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Alabama Bankruptcy Statistics

Data from the Federal Judicial Center (FJC), covering 1,743 consumer bankruptcy cases filed in Alabama.

ChapterCases FiledDischarge RateDismissal Rate

Chapter 7478Insufficient dataInsufficient data

Chapter 131,265Insufficient dataInsufficient data

Chapter 7 Share

27.4% of consumer filings

Chapter 13 Share

72.6% of consumer filings

Discharge and dismissal rates reflect resolved cases only. Pending cases are excluded. Source: FJC Integrated Database.

Filing Bankruptcy in Alabama

Districts

Alabama has 3 federal bankruptcy districts. The district where you file depends on where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. Each district may have different local rules, no-look fee amounts, and trustee practices.

Filing Costs

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338. Chapter 13: $313. Fee waivers are available for filers below 150% of the poverty line. Installment payments available for all filers. Full cost breakdown.

Before You File

Complete credit counseling from an approved provider (required within 180 days before filing).

Determine your chapter -- Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 comparison.

Gather income documentation for the last 6 full calendar months.

List all debts, assets, income, and expenses for the bankruptcy schedules.

Research Alabama exemptions to understand what property you can keep.

After You File

The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and lawsuits.

Attend the 341 meeting of creditors (usually 30-45 days after filing).

Complete a debtor education course (required before discharge).

Receive your discharge (Chapter 7: ~60-90 days after 341 meeting; Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan).

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Alabama?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees in Alabama typically range from $1,000 to $3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $5,000 for Chapter 13. You can also file without an attorney (pro se) and request to pay the filing fee in installments.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Alabama?

The means test compares your household income to Alabama's median. For a single person, the current threshold is approximately $56,050 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $99,208. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

What property can I keep in Alabama bankruptcy?

Alabama requires filers to use state exemptions only. Key exemptions to research include the homestead exemption (how much home equity you can protect), vehicle exemption, personal property exemption, and any wildcard exemption. Check Alabama statutes for current amounts.

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit in Alabama?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, many people begin rebuilding credit within 1-2 years of discharge and can qualify for a mortgage within 2-4 years.

Can I file bankruptcy again in Alabama?

Yes, but there are waiting periods between discharges. Chapter 7 to Chapter 7: 8 years. Chapter 7 to Chapter 13: 4 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 13: 2 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 7: 6 years (unless you paid 100% or 70%+ with good faith). Use the free discharge eligibility screener to check your dates.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within Alabama?

Alabama has 3 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district. Check local court websites for county-to-district lookups.

What debts cannot be discharged in bankruptcy?

Most debts are dischargeable, but exceptions include most student loans (unless you prove undue hardship), child support and alimony, recent tax debts (generally last 3 years), debts from fraud or willful injury, DUI-related debts, and criminal fines. See nondischargeable.org for the full list.

Bankruptcy in Nearby States

Mississippi · Tennessee · Georgia · Florida

Free Bankruptcy Tools

From the Open Bankruptcy Project -- free information, no strings.

Part of the Open Bankruptcy Project | Free information, no strings.

## Bankruptcy in Alaska: Filing Guide, Exemptions, and Statistics [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcyexemptionsbystate.com/states/alaska.html

Exemptions by State | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Bankruptcy in Alaska

Complete guide: exemptions, means test income limits, filing costs, and real case outcome data for Alaska.

Important: This page provides educational information about bankruptcy in Alaska. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

1

Bankruptcy District

$73,866

Median Income (1 person)

Yes

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Alaska Exemptions

Federal Exemptions Available

Filers in Alaska can choose between state and federal bankruptcy exemptions. Compare both sets carefully -- the federal wildcard exemption (up to $14,875) may protect more personal property, while Alaska state exemptions may offer stronger homestead protection.

Homestead Exemption

Protects equity in your primary residence. Amounts vary by state. Check Alaska homestead exemption amounts.

Vehicle Exemption

Protects equity in your car. Most states protect $2,000 to $7,500. See vehicle exemption details.

Retirement Accounts

401(k)s, IRAs, and pensions are generally fully exempt in bankruptcy. See retirement exemptions.

Wildcard Exemption

Applies to any property. Not all states offer this. See wildcard exemptions.

Alaska Means Test Income Limits

If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 of the means test and likely qualify for Chapter 7.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$73,866$6,156

2 persons$96,026$8,002

3 persons$112,276$9,356

4 persons$130,743$10,895

5 persons$141,843$11,820

6 persons$152,943$12,745

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Filing Bankruptcy in Alaska

Districts

Alaska has a single federal bankruptcy district. All cases are filed in this one court, which simplifies the process.

Filing Costs

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338. Chapter 13: $313. Fee waivers are available for filers below 150% of the poverty line. Installment payments available for all filers. Full cost breakdown.

Before You File

Complete credit counseling from an approved provider (required within 180 days before filing).

Determine your chapter -- Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 comparison.

Gather income documentation for the last 6 full calendar months.

List all debts, assets, income, and expenses for the bankruptcy schedules.

Research Alaska exemptions to understand what property you can keep.

After You File

The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and lawsuits.

Attend the 341 meeting of creditors (usually 30-45 days after filing).

Complete a debtor education course (required before discharge).

Receive your discharge (Chapter 7: ~60-90 days after 341 meeting; Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan).

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Alaska?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees in Alaska typically range from $1,000 to $3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $5,000 for Chapter 13. You can also file without an attorney (pro se) and request to pay the filing fee in installments.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Alaska?

The means test compares your household income to Alaska's median. For a single person, the current threshold is approximately $73,866 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $130,743. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Can I use federal exemptions in Alaska?

Yes. Alaska allows you to choose between state exemptions and federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d). You must pick one complete set -- you cannot mix individual exemptions from both. Compare homestead, vehicle, and wildcard amounts under each system before deciding.

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit in Alaska?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, many people begin rebuilding credit within 1-2 years of discharge and can qualify for a mortgage within 2-4 years.

Can I file bankruptcy again in Alaska?

Yes, but there are waiting periods between discharges. Chapter 7 to Chapter 7: 8 years. Chapter 7 to Chapter 13: 4 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 13: 2 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 7: 6 years (unless you paid 100% or 70%+ with good faith). Use the free discharge eligibility screener to check your dates.

What debts cannot be discharged in bankruptcy?

Most debts are dischargeable, but exceptions include most student loans (unless you prove undue hardship), child support and alimony, recent tax debts (generally last 3 years), debts from fraud or willful injury, DUI-related debts, and criminal fines. See nondischargeable.org for the full list.

Filing Bankruptcy in Arkansas

Districts

Receive your discharge (Chapter 7: ~60-90 days after 341 meeting; Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan).

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Arkansas?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, many people begin rebuilding credit within 1-2 years of discharge and can qualify for a mortgage within 2-4 years.

Can I file bankruptcy again in Arkansas?

Yes, but there are waiting periods between discharges. Chapter 7 to Chapter 7: 8 years. Chapter 7 to Chapter 13: 4 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 13: 2 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 7: 6 years (unless you paid 100% or 70%+ with good faith). Use the free discharge eligibility screener to check your dates.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within Arkansas?

Arkansas has 2 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district. Check local court websites for county-to-district lookups.

What debts cannot be discharged in bankruptcy?

Most debts are dischargeable, but exceptions include most student loans (unless you prove undue hardship), child support and alimony, recent tax debts (generally last 3 years), debts from fraud or willful injury, DUI-related debts, and criminal fines. See nondischargeable.org for the full list.

Bankruptcy in Nearby States

Missouri · Tennessee · Mississippi · Louisiana

## Bankruptcy in California: Filing Guide, Exemptions, and Statistics [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcyexemptionsbystate.com/states/california.html

Exemptions by State | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Bankruptcy in California

Complete guide: exemptions, means test income limits, filing costs, and real case outcome data for California.

Important: This page provides educational information about bankruptcy in California. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

4

Bankruptcy Districts

$79,253

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

California Exemptions

State Exemptions Only

California requires bankruptcy filers to use state exemptions exclusively. Federal exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available. Research California's homestead, vehicle, personal property, and wildcard exemptions before filing.

Homestead Exemption

Protects equity in your primary residence. Amounts vary by state. Check California homestead exemption amounts.

Vehicle Exemption

Protects equity in your car. Most states protect $2,000 to $7,500. See vehicle exemption details.

Retirement Accounts

401(k)s, IRAs, and pensions are generally fully exempt in bankruptcy. See retirement exemptions.

Wildcard Exemption

Applies to any property. Not all states offer this. See wildcard exemptions.

California Means Test Income Limits

If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 of the means test and likely qualify for Chapter 7.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$79,253$6,604

2 persons$103,029$8,586

3 persons$120,465$10,039

4 persons$140,278$11,690

5 persons$151,378$12,615

6 persons$162,478$13,540

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

California Bankruptcy Statistics

Data from the Federal Judicial Center (FJC), covering 6,723 consumer bankruptcy cases filed in California.

ChapterCases FiledDischarge RateDismissal Rate

Chapter 75,80898.4%1.6%

Chapter 1391538.8%61.2%

Chapter 7 Share

86.4% of consumer filings

Chapter 13 Share

13.6% of consumer filings

Discharge and dismissal rates reflect resolved cases only. Pending cases are excluded. Source: FJC Integrated Database.

Filing Bankruptcy in California

Districts

California has 4 federal bankruptcy districts. The district where you file depends on where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. Each district may have different local rules, no-look fee amounts, and trustee practices.

Filing Costs

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338. Chapter 13: $313. Fee waivers are available for filers below 150% of the poverty line. Installment payments available for all filers. Full cost breakdown.

Before You File

Complete credit counseling from an approved provider (required within 180 days before filing).

Determine your chapter -- Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 comparison.

Gather income documentation for the last 6 full calendar months.

List all debts, assets, income, and expenses for the bankruptcy schedules.

Research California exemptions to understand what property you can keep.

After You File

The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and lawsuits.

Attend the 341 meeting of creditors (usually 30-45 days after filing).

Complete a debtor education course (required before discharge).

Receive your discharge (Chapter 7: ~60-90 days after 341 meeting; Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan).

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in California?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees in California typically range from $1,000 to $3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $5,000 for Chapter 13. You can also file without an attorney (pro se) and request to pay the filing fee in installments.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in California?

The means test compares your household income to California's median. For a single person, the current threshold is approximately $79,253 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $140,278. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

What property can I keep in California bankruptcy?

California requires filers to use state exemptions only. Key exemptions to research include the homestead exemption (how much home equity you can protect), vehicle exemption, personal property exemption, and any wildcard exemption. Check California statutes for current amounts.

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit in California?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, many people begin rebuilding credit within 1-2 years of discharge and can qualify for a mortgage within 2-4 years.

Can I file bankruptcy again in California?

Yes, but there are waiting periods between discharges. Chapter 7 to Chapter 7: 8 years. Chapter 7 to Chapter 13: 4 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 13: 2 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 7: 6 years (unless you paid 100% or 70%+ with good faith). Use the free discharge eligibility screener to check your dates.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within California?

California has 4 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district. Check local court websites for county-to-district lookups.

What debts cannot be discharged in bankruptcy?

Most debts are dischargeable, but exceptions include most student loans (unless you prove undue hardship), child support and alimony, recent tax debts (generally last 3 years), debts from fraud or willful injury, DUI-related debts, and criminal fines. See nondischargeable.org for the full list.

Bankruptcy in Nearby States

Oregon · Nevada · Arizona

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Colorado Exemptions

State Exemptions Only

Colorado requires bankruptcy filers to use state exemptions exclusively. Federal exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available. Research Colorado's homestead, vehicle, personal property, and wildcard exemptions before filing.

Homestead Exemption

Protects equity in your primary residence. Amounts vary by state. Check Colorado homestead exemption amounts.

Vehicle Exemption

Protects equity in your car. Most states protect $2,000 to $7,500. See vehicle exemption details.

Retirement Accounts

401(k)s, IRAs, and pensions are generally fully exempt in bankruptcy. See retirement exemptions.

Applies to any property. Not all states offer this. See wildcard exemptions.

Filing Bankruptcy in Colorado

Districts

Colorado has a single federal bankruptcy district. All cases are filed in this one court, which simplifies the process.

Filing Costs

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338. Chapter 13: $313. Fee waivers are available for filers below 150% of the poverty line. Installment payments available for all filers. Full cost breakdown.

Before You File

Complete credit counseling from an approved provider (required within 180 days before filing).

Determine your chapter -- Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 comparison.

Gather income documentation for the last 6 full calendar months.

List all debts, assets, income, and expenses for the bankruptcy schedules.

Research Colorado exemptions to understand what property you can keep.

After You File

The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and lawsuits.

Attend the 341 meeting of creditors (usually 30-45 days after filing).

Complete a debtor education course (required before discharge).

Receive your discharge (Chapter 7: ~60-90 days after 341 meeting; Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan).

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Colorado?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees in Colorado typically range from $1,000 to $3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $5,000 for Chapter 13. You can also file without an attorney (pro se) and request to pay the filing fee in installments.

The means test compares your household income to Colorado's median. For a single person, the current threshold is approximately $82,700 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $146,379. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

What property can I keep in Colorado bankruptcy?

Colorado requires filers to use state exemptions only. Key exemptions to research include the homestead exemption (how much home equity you can protect), vehicle exemption, personal property exemption, and any wildcard exemption. Check Colorado statutes for current amounts.

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit in Colorado?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, many people begin rebuilding credit within 1-2 years of discharge and can qualify for a mortgage within 2-4 years.

Can I file bankruptcy again in Colorado?

Yes, but there are waiting periods between discharges. Chapter 7 to Chapter 7: 8 years. Chapter 7 to Chapter 13: 4 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 13: 2 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 7: 6 years (unless you paid 100% or 70%+ with good faith). Use the free discharge eligibility screener to check your dates.

Most debts are dischargeable, but exceptions include most student loans (unless you prove undue hardship), child support and alimony, recent tax debts (generally last 3 years), debts from fraud or willful injury, DUI-related debts, and criminal fines. See nondischargeable.org for the full list.

## Bankruptcy in Connecticut: Filing Guide, Exemptions, and Statistics [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcyexemptionsbystate.com/states/connecticut.html

Exemptions by State | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Bankruptcy in Connecticut

Complete guide: exemptions, means test income limits, filing costs, and real case outcome data for Connecticut.

Important: This page provides educational information about bankruptcy in Connecticut. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

1

Bankruptcy District

$87,411

Median Income (1 person)

Yes

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Connecticut Exemptions

Federal Exemptions Available

Filers in Connecticut can choose between state and federal bankruptcy exemptions. Compare both sets carefully -- the federal wildcard exemption (up to $14,875) may protect more personal property, while Connecticut state exemptions may offer stronger homestead protection.

Homestead Exemption

Protects equity in your primary residence. Amounts vary by state. Check Connecticut homestead exemption amounts.

Vehicle Exemption

Protects equity in your car. Most states protect $2,000 to $7,500. See vehicle exemption details.

Retirement Accounts

401(k)s, IRAs, and pensions are generally fully exempt in bankruptcy. See retirement exemptions.

Wildcard Exemption

Applies to any property. Not all states offer this. See wildcard exemptions.

Connecticut Means Test Income Limits

If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 of the means test and likely qualify for Chapter 7.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$87,411$7,284

2 persons$113,634$9,470

3 persons$132,865$11,072

4 persons$154,717$12,893

5 persons$165,817$13,818

6 persons$176,917$14,743

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Connecticut Bankruptcy Statistics

Data from the Federal Judicial Center (FJC), covering 93 consumer bankruptcy cases filed in Connecticut.

ChapterCases FiledDischarge RateDismissal Rate

Chapter 77495.2%4.8%

Chapter 1319Insufficient dataInsufficient data

Chapter 7 Share

79.6% of consumer filings

Chapter 13 Share

20.4% of consumer filings

Discharge and dismissal rates reflect resolved cases only. Pending cases are excluded. Source: FJC Integrated Database.

Filing Bankruptcy in Connecticut

Districts

Connecticut has a single federal bankruptcy district. All cases are filed in this one court, which simplifies the process.

Filing Costs

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338. Chapter 13: $313. Fee waivers are available for filers below 150% of the poverty line. Installment payments available for all filers. Full cost breakdown.

Before You File

Complete credit counseling from an approved provider (required within 180 days before filing).

Determine your chapter -- Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 comparison.

Gather income documentation for the last 6 full calendar months.

List all debts, assets, income, and expenses for the bankruptcy schedules.

Research Connecticut exemptions to understand what property you can keep.

After You File

The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and lawsuits.

Attend the 341 meeting of creditors (usually 30-45 days after filing).

Complete a debtor education course (required before discharge).

Receive your discharge (Chapter 7: ~60-90 days after 341 meeting; Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan).

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Connecticut?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees in Connecticut typically range from $1,000 to $3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $5,000 for Chapter 13. You can also file without an attorney (pro se) and request to pay the filing fee in installments.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Connecticut?

The means test compares your household income to Connecticut's median. For a single person, the current threshold is approximately $87,411 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $154,717. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Can I use federal exemptions in Connecticut?

Yes. Connecticut allows you to choose between state exemptions and federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d). You must pick one complete set -- you cannot mix individual exemptions from both. Compare homestead, vehicle, and wildcard amounts under each system before deciding.

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit in Connecticut?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, many people begin rebuilding credit within 1-2 years of discharge and can qualify for a mortgage within 2-4 years.

Can I file bankruptcy again in Connecticut?

Yes, but there are waiting periods between discharges. Chapter 7 to Chapter 7: 8 years. Chapter 7 to Chapter 13: 4 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 13: 2 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 7: 6 years (unless you paid 100% or 70%+ with good faith). Use the free discharge eligibility screener to check your dates.

What debts cannot be discharged in bankruptcy?

Most debts are dischargeable, but exceptions include most student loans (unless you prove undue hardship), child support and alimony, recent tax debts (generally last 3 years), debts from fraud or willful injury, DUI-related debts, and criminal fines. See nondischargeable.org for the full list.

Bankruptcy in Florida

Complete guide: exemptions, means test income limits, filing costs, and real case outcome data for Florida.

Important: This page provides educational information about bankruptcy in Florida. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

3

Bankruptcy Districts

$69,876

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Florida Exemptions

State Exemptions Only

Florida requires bankruptcy filers to use state exemptions exclusively. Federal exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available. Research Florida's homestead, vehicle, personal property, and wildcard exemptions before filing.

Homestead Exemption

Protects equity in your primary residence. Amounts vary by state. Check Florida homestead exemption amounts.

Vehicle Exemption

Protects equity in your car. Most states protect $2,000 to $7,500. See vehicle exemption details.

Retirement Accounts

401(k)s, IRAs, and pensions are generally fully exempt in bankruptcy. See retirement exemptions.

Wildcard Exemption

Applies to any property. Not all states offer this. See wildcard exemptions.

Florida Means Test Income Limits

If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 of the means test and likely qualify for Chapter 7.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$69,876$5,823

2 persons$90,839$7,570

3 persons$106,212$8,851

4 persons$123,681$10,307

5 persons$134,781$11,232

6 persons$145,881$12,157

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Florida Bankruptcy Statistics

Data from the Federal Judicial Center (FJC), covering 46,008 consumer bankruptcy cases filed in Florida.

ChapterCases FiledDischarge RateDismissal Rate

Chapter 724,38994.2%5.1%

Chapter 1321,61951.3%48.6%

Chapter 7 Share

53.0% of consumer filings

Chapter 13 Share

47.0% of consumer filings

Discharge and dismissal rates reflect resolved cases only. Pending cases are excluded. Source: FJC Integrated Database.

Filing Bankruptcy in Florida

Districts

Florida has 3 federal bankruptcy districts. The district where you file depends on where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. Each district may have different local rules, no-look fee amounts, and trustee practices.

Filing Costs

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338. Chapter 13: $313. Fee waivers are available for filers below 150% of the poverty line. Installment payments available for all filers. Full cost breakdown.

Before You File

Complete credit counseling from an approved provider (required within 180 days before filing).

Determine your chapter -- Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 comparison.

Gather income documentation for the last 6 full calendar months.

List all debts, assets, income, and expenses for the bankruptcy schedules.

Research Florida exemptions to understand what property you can keep.

After You File

The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and lawsuits.

Attend the 341 meeting of creditors (usually 30-45 days after filing).

Complete a debtor education course (required before discharge).

Receive your discharge (Chapter 7: ~60-90 days after 341 meeting; Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan).

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Florida?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees in Florida typically range from $1,000 to $3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $5,000 for Chapter 13. You can also file without an attorney (pro se) and request to pay the filing fee in installments.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Florida?

The means test compares your household income to Florida's median. For a single person, the current threshold is approximately $69,876 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $123,681. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

What property can I keep in Florida bankruptcy?

Florida requires filers to use state exemptions only. Key exemptions to research include the homestead exemption (how much home equity you can protect), vehicle exemption, personal property exemption, and any wildcard exemption. Check Florida statutes for current amounts.

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit in Florida?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, many people begin rebuilding credit within 1-2 years of discharge and can qualify for a mortgage within 2-4 years.

Can I file bankruptcy again in Florida?

Yes, but there are waiting periods between discharges. Chapter 7 to Chapter 7: 8 years. Chapter 7 to Chapter 13: 4 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 13: 2 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 7: 6 years (unless you paid 100% or 70%+ with good faith). Use the free discharge eligibility screener to check your dates.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within Florida?

Florida has 3 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district. Check local court websites for county-to-district lookups.

What debts cannot be discharged in bankruptcy?

Most debts are dischargeable, but exceptions include most student loans (unless you prove undue hardship), child support and alimony, recent tax debts (generally last 3 years), debts from fraud or willful injury, DUI-related debts, and criminal fines. See nondischargeable.org for the full list.

Bankruptcy in Nearby States

Georgia · Alabama

Free Bankruptcy Tools

From the Open Bankruptcy Project -- free information, no strings.

Bankruptcy in Georgia

Complete guide: exemptions, means test income limits, filing costs, and real case outcome data for Georgia.

Important: This page provides educational information about bankruptcy in Georgia. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

Bankruptcy Districts

$65,628

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Georgia Exemptions

State Exemptions Only

Georgia requires bankruptcy filers to use state exemptions exclusively. Federal exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available. Research Georgia's homestead, vehicle, personal property, and wildcard exemptions before filing.

Homestead Exemption

Protects equity in your primary residence. Amounts vary by state. Check Georgia homestead exemption amounts.

Vehicle Exemption

Protects equity in your car. Most states protect $2,000 to $7,500. See vehicle exemption details.

Retirement Accounts

401(k)s, IRAs, and pensions are generally fully exempt in bankruptcy. See retirement exemptions.

Wildcard Exemption

Applies to any property. Not all states offer this. See wildcard exemptions.

Filing Bankruptcy in Georgia

Districts

Georgia has 3 federal bankruptcy districts. The district where you file depends on where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. Each district may have different local rules, no-look fee amounts, and trustee practices.

Filing Costs

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338. Chapter 13: $313. Fee waivers are available for filers below 150% of the poverty line. Installment payments available for all filers. Full cost breakdown.

Before You File

Complete credit counseling from an approved provider (required within 180 days before filing).

Determine your chapter -- Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 comparison.

Gather income documentation for the last 6 full calendar months.

List all debts, assets, income, and expenses for the bankruptcy schedules.

Research Georgia exemptions to understand what property you can keep.

After You File

The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and lawsuits.

Attend the 341 meeting of creditors (usually 30-45 days after filing).

Complete a debtor education course (required before discharge).

Receive your discharge (Chapter 7: ~60-90 days after 341 meeting; Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan).

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Georgia?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees in Georgia typically range from $1,000 to $3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $5,000 for Chapter 13. You can also file without an attorney (pro se) and request to pay the filing fee in installments.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Georgia?

The means test compares your household income to Georgia's median. For a single person, the current threshold is approximately $65,628 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $116,162. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

What property can I keep in Georgia bankruptcy?

Georgia requires filers to use state exemptions only. Key exemptions to research include the homestead exemption (how much home equity you can protect), vehicle exemption, personal property exemption, and any wildcard exemption. Check Georgia statutes for current amounts.

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit in Georgia?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, many people begin rebuilding credit within 1-2 years of discharge and can qualify for a mortgage within 2-4 years.

Can I file bankruptcy again in Georgia?

Yes, but there are waiting periods between discharges. Chapter 7 to Chapter 7: 8 years. Chapter 7 to Chapter 13: 4 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 13: 2 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 7: 6 years (unless you paid 100% or 70%+ with good faith). Use the free discharge eligibility screener to check your dates.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within Georgia?

Georgia has 3 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district. Check local court websites for county-to-district lookups.

What debts cannot be discharged in bankruptcy?

Most debts are dischargeable, but exceptions include most student loans (unless you prove undue hardship), child support and alimony, recent tax debts (generally last 3 years), debts from fraud or willful injury, DUI-related debts, and criminal fines. See nondischargeable.org for the full list.

## Bankruptcy in Hawaii: Filing Guide, Exemptions, and Statistics [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcyexemptionsbystate.com/states/hawaii.html

Exemptions by State | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Bankruptcy in Hawaii

Complete guide: exemptions, means test income limits, filing costs, and real case outcome data for Hawaii.

Important: This page provides educational information about bankruptcy in Hawaii. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

1

Bankruptcy District

$86,427

Median Income (1 person)

Yes

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Hawaii Exemptions

Federal Exemptions Available

Filers in Hawaii can choose between state and federal bankruptcy exemptions. Compare both sets carefully -- the federal wildcard exemption (up to $14,875) may protect more personal property, while Hawaii state exemptions may offer stronger homestead protection.

Homestead Exemption

Protects equity in your primary residence. Amounts vary by state. Check Hawaii homestead exemption amounts.

Vehicle Exemption

Protects equity in your car. Most states protect $2,000 to $7,500. See vehicle exemption details.

Retirement Accounts

401(k)s, IRAs, and pensions are generally fully exempt in bankruptcy. See retirement exemptions.

Wildcard Exemption

Applies to any property. Not all states offer this. See wildcard exemptions.

Hawaii Means Test Income Limits

If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 of the means test and likely qualify for Chapter 7.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$86,427$7,202

2 persons$112,355$9,363

3 persons$131,369$10,947

4 persons$152,976$12,748

5 persons$164,076$13,673

6 persons$175,176$14,598

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Filing Bankruptcy in Hawaii

Districts

Hawaii has a single federal bankruptcy district. All cases are filed in this one court, which simplifies the process.

Filing Costs

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338. Chapter 13: $313. Fee waivers are available for filers below 150% of the poverty line. Installment payments available for all filers. Full cost breakdown.

Before You File

Complete credit counseling from an approved provider (required within 180 days before filing).

Determine your chapter -- Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 comparison.

Gather income documentation for the last 6 full calendar months.

List all debts, assets, income, and expenses for the bankruptcy schedules.

Research Hawaii exemptions to understand what property you can keep.

After You File

The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and lawsuits.

Attend the 341 meeting of creditors (usually 30-45 days after filing).

Complete a debtor education course (required before discharge).

Receive your discharge (Chapter 7: ~60-90 days after 341 meeting; Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan).

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Hawaii?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees in Hawaii typically range from $1,000 to $3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $5,000 for Chapter 13. You can also file without an attorney (pro se) and request to pay the filing fee in installments.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Hawaii?

The means test compares your household income to Hawaii's median. For a single person, the current threshold is approximately $86,427 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $152,976. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Can I use federal exemptions in Hawaii?

Yes. Hawaii allows you to choose between state exemptions and federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d). You must pick one complete set -- you cannot mix individual exemptions from both. Compare homestead, vehicle, and wildcard amounts under each system before deciding.

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit in Hawaii?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, many people begin rebuilding credit within 1-2 years of discharge and can qualify for a mortgage within 2-4 years.

Can I file bankruptcy again in Hawaii?

Yes, but there are waiting periods between discharges. Chapter 7 to Chapter 7: 8 years. Chapter 7 to Chapter 13: 4 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 13: 2 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 7: 6 years (unless you paid 100% or 70%+ with good faith). Use the free discharge eligibility screener to check your dates.

What debts cannot be discharged in bankruptcy?

Most debts are dischargeable, but exceptions include most student loans (unless you prove undue hardship), child support and alimony, recent tax debts (generally last 3 years), debts from fraud or willful injury, DUI-related debts, and criminal fines. See nondischargeable.org for the full list.

## Bankruptcy in Indiana: Filing Guide, Exemptions, and Statistics [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcyexemptionsbystate.com/states/indiana.html

Exemptions by State | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Bankruptcy in Indiana

Complete guide: exemptions, means test income limits, filing costs, and real case outcome data for Indiana.

Important: This page provides educational information about bankruptcy in Indiana. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

2

Bankruptcy Districts

$63,399

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Indiana Exemptions

State Exemptions Only

Indiana requires bankruptcy filers to use state exemptions exclusively. Federal exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available. Research Indiana's homestead, vehicle, personal property, and wildcard exemptions before filing.

Homestead Exemption

Protects equity in your primary residence. Amounts vary by state. Check Indiana homestead exemption amounts.

Vehicle Exemption

Protects equity in your car. Most states protect $2,000 to $7,500. See vehicle exemption details.

Retirement Accounts

401(k)s, IRAs, and pensions are generally fully exempt in bankruptcy. See retirement exemptions.

Wildcard Exemption

Applies to any property. Not all states offer this. See wildcard exemptions.

Indiana Means Test Income Limits

If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 of the means test and likely qualify for Chapter 7.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$63,399$5,283

2 persons$82,419$6,868

3 persons$96,366$8,030

4 persons$112,216$9,351

5 persons$123,316$10,276

6 persons$134,416$11,201

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Indiana Bankruptcy Statistics

Data from the Federal Judicial Center (FJC), covering 3,325 consumer bankruptcy cases filed in Indiana.

ChapterCases FiledDischarge RateDismissal Rate

Chapter 779297.5%2.0%

Chapter 132,53369.8%29.5%

Chapter 7 Share

23.8% of consumer filings

Chapter 13 Share

76.2% of consumer filings

Discharge and dismissal rates reflect resolved cases only. Pending cases are excluded. Source: FJC Integrated Database.

Filing Bankruptcy in Indiana

Districts

Indiana has 2 federal bankruptcy districts. The district where you file depends on where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. Each district may have different local rules, no-look fee amounts, and trustee practices.

Filing Costs

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338. Chapter 13: $313. Fee waivers are available for filers below 150% of the poverty line. Installment payments available for all filers. Full cost breakdown.

Before You File

Complete credit counseling from an approved provider (required within 180 days before filing).

Determine your chapter -- Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 comparison.

Gather income documentation for the last 6 full calendar months.

List all debts, assets, income, and expenses for the bankruptcy schedules.

Research Indiana exemptions to understand what property you can keep.

After You File

The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and lawsuits.

Attend the 341 meeting of creditors (usually 30-45 days after filing).

Complete a debtor education course (required before discharge).

Receive your discharge (Chapter 7: ~60-90 days after 341 meeting; Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan).

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Indiana?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees in Indiana typically range from $1,000 to $3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $5,000 for Chapter 13. You can also file without an attorney (pro se) and request to pay the filing fee in installments.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Indiana?

The means test compares your household income to Indiana's median. For a single person, the current threshold is approximately $63,399 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $112,216. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

What property can I keep in Indiana bankruptcy?

Indiana requires filers to use state exemptions only. Key exemptions to research include the homestead exemption (how much home equity you can protect), vehicle exemption, personal property exemption, and any wildcard exemption. Check Indiana statutes for current amounts.

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit in Indiana?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, many people begin rebuilding credit within 1-2 years of discharge and can qualify for a mortgage within 2-4 years.

Can I file bankruptcy again in Indiana?

Yes, but there are waiting periods between discharges. Chapter 7 to Chapter 7: 8 years. Chapter 7 to Chapter 13: 4 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 13: 2 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 7: 6 years (unless you paid 100% or 70%+ with good faith). Use the free discharge eligibility screener to check your dates.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within Indiana?

Indiana has 2 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district. Check local court websites for county-to-district lookups.

What debts cannot be discharged in bankruptcy?

Most debts are dischargeable, but exceptions include most student loans (unless you prove undue hardship), child support and alimony, recent tax debts (generally last 3 years), debts from fraud or willful injury, DUI-related debts, and criminal fines. See nondischargeable.org for the full list.

Bankruptcy in Nearby States

Illinois · Michigan · Ohio · Kentucky

Free Bankruptcy Tools

From the Open Bankruptcy Project -- free information, no strings.

Discharge Screener
Means Test Calculator

Bankruptcy Exemptions Guide

Bankruptcy Lien Guide

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy

Keep Your House in Bankruptcy

Part of the Open Bankruptcy Project | Free information, no strings.

341 Meeting ·
523(a) Exceptions ·
727(a)(8) ·
Ch 7 vs 13 ·

Source: https://bankruptcyexemptionsbystate.com/states/iowa.html

Exemptions by State | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Bankruptcy in Iowa

Complete guide: exemptions, means test income limits, filing costs, and real case outcome data for Iowa.

Important: This page provides educational information about bankruptcy in Iowa. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

2

Bankruptcy Districts

$68,223

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Iowa Exemptions

State Exemptions Only

Iowa requires bankruptcy filers to use state exemptions exclusively. Federal exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available. Research Iowa's homestead, vehicle, personal property, and wildcard exemptions before filing.

Homestead Exemption

Protects equity in your primary residence. Amounts vary by state. Check Iowa homestead exemption amounts.

Vehicle Exemption

Protects equity in your car. Most states protect $2,000 to $7,500. See vehicle exemption details.

Retirement Accounts

401(k)s, IRAs, and pensions are generally fully exempt in bankruptcy. See retirement exemptions.

Wildcard Exemption

Applies to any property. Not all states offer this. See wildcard exemptions.

Iowa Means Test Income Limits

If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 of the means test and likely qualify for Chapter 7.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

2 persons$88,690$7,391

3 persons$103,699$8,642

4 persons$120,755$10,063

5 persons$131,855$10,988

6 persons$142,955$11,913

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.
