Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Filing Bankruptcy in Iowa

Districts

Iowa has 2 federal bankruptcy districts. The district where you file depends on where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. Each district may have different local rules, no-look fee amounts, and trustee practices.

Filing Costs

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338. Chapter 13: $313. Fee waivers are available for filers below 150% of the poverty line. Installment payments available for all filers. Full cost breakdown.

Before You File

Complete credit counseling from an approved provider (required within 180 days before filing).

Determine your chapter -- Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 comparison.

Gather income documentation for the last 6 full calendar months.

List all debts, assets, income, and expenses for the bankruptcy schedules.

Research Iowa exemptions to understand what property you can keep.

After You File

The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and lawsuits.

Attend the 341 meeting of creditors (usually 30-45 days after filing).

Complete a debtor education course (required before discharge).

Receive your discharge (Chapter 7: ~60-90 days after 341 meeting; Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan).

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Iowa?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees in Iowa typically range from $1,000 to $3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $5,000 for Chapter 13. You can also file without an attorney (pro se) and request to pay the filing fee in installments.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Iowa?

The means test compares your household income to Iowa's median. For a single person, the current threshold is approximately $68,223 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $120,755. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

What property can I keep in Iowa bankruptcy?

Iowa requires filers to use state exemptions only. Key exemptions to research include the homestead exemption (how much home equity you can protect), vehicle exemption, personal property exemption, and any wildcard exemption. Check Iowa statutes for current amounts.

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit in Iowa?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, many people begin rebuilding credit within 1-2 years of discharge and can qualify for a mortgage within 2-4 years.

Can I file bankruptcy again in Iowa?

Yes, but there are waiting periods between discharges. Chapter 7 to Chapter 7: 8 years. Chapter 7 to Chapter 13: 4 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 13: 2 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 7: 6 years (unless you paid 100% or 70%+ with good faith). Use the free discharge eligibility screener to check your dates.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within Iowa?

Iowa has 2 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district. Check local court websites for county-to-district lookups.

What debts cannot be discharged in bankruptcy?

Most debts are dischargeable, but exceptions include most student loans (unless you prove undue hardship), child support and alimony, recent tax debts (generally last 3 years), debts from fraud or willful injury, DUI-related debts, and criminal fines. See nondischargeable.org for the full list.

## Bankruptcy in Kansas: Filing Guide, Exemptions, and Statistics [2026]

Exemptions by State | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Bankruptcy in Kansas

Complete guide: exemptions, means test income limits, filing costs, and real case outcome data for Kansas.

Important: This page provides educational information about bankruptcy in Kansas. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

1

Bankruptcy District

$69,197

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Kansas Exemptions

State Exemptions Only

Kansas requires bankruptcy filers to use state exemptions exclusively. Federal exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available. Research Kansas's homestead, vehicle, personal property, and wildcard exemptions before filing.

Homestead Exemption

Protects equity in your primary residence. Amounts vary by state. Check Kansas homestead exemption amounts.

Vehicle Exemption

Protects equity in your car. Most states protect $2,000 to $7,500. See vehicle exemption details.

Retirement Accounts

401(k)s, IRAs, and pensions are generally fully exempt in bankruptcy. See retirement exemptions.

Wildcard Exemption

Applies to any property. Not all states offer this. See wildcard exemptions.

If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 of the means test and likely qualify for Chapter 7.

Kansas Bankruptcy Statistics

45.7% of consumer filings

Chapter 13 Share

54.3% of consumer filings

Discharge and dismissal rates reflect resolved cases only. Pending cases are excluded. Source: FJC Integrated Database.

Filing Bankruptcy in Kansas

Districts

Kansas has a single federal bankruptcy district. All cases are filed in this one court, which simplifies the process.

Filing Costs

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338. Chapter 13: $313. Fee waivers are available for filers below 150% of the poverty line. Installment payments available for all filers. Full cost breakdown.

Before You File

Complete credit counseling from an approved provider (required within 180 days before filing).

Determine your chapter -- Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 comparison.

Gather income documentation for the last 6 full calendar months.

List all debts, assets, income, and expenses for the bankruptcy schedules.

Research Kansas exemptions to understand what property you can keep.

After You File

The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and lawsuits.

Attend the 341 meeting of creditors (usually 30-45 days after filing).

Complete a debtor education course (required before discharge).

Receive your discharge (Chapter 7: ~60-90 days after 341 meeting; Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan).

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Kansas?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees in Kansas typically range from $1,000 to $3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $5,000 for Chapter 13. You can also file without an attorney (pro se) and request to pay the filing fee in installments.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Kansas?

The means test compares your household income to Kansas's median. For a single person, the current threshold is approximately $69,197 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $122,479. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

What property can I keep in Kansas bankruptcy?

Kansas requires filers to use state exemptions only. Key exemptions to research include the homestead exemption (how much home equity you can protect), vehicle exemption, personal property exemption, and any wildcard exemption. Check Kansas statutes for current amounts.

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit in Kansas?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, many people begin rebuilding credit within 1-2 years of discharge and can qualify for a mortgage within 2-4 years.

Can I file bankruptcy again in Kansas?

Yes, but there are waiting periods between discharges. Chapter 7 to Chapter 7: 8 years. Chapter 7 to Chapter 13: 4 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 13: 2 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 7: 6 years (unless you paid 100% or 70%+ with good faith). Use the free discharge eligibility screener to check your dates.

What debts cannot be discharged in bankruptcy?

Most debts are dischargeable, but exceptions include most student loans (unless you prove undue hardship), child support and alimony, recent tax debts (generally last 3 years), debts from fraud or willful injury, DUI-related debts, and criminal fines. See nondischargeable.org for the full list.

## Bankruptcy in Kentucky: Filing Guide, Exemptions, and Statistics [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcyexemptionsbystate.com/states/kentucky.html

Exemptions by State | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Bankruptcy in Kentucky

Complete guide: exemptions, means test income limits, filing costs, and real case outcome data for Kentucky.

Important: This page provides educational information about bankruptcy in Kentucky. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

2

Bankruptcy Districts

$58,632

Median Income (1 person)

Yes

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Kentucky Exemptions

Federal Exemptions Available

Filers in Kentucky can choose between state and federal bankruptcy exemptions. Compare both sets carefully -- the federal wildcard exemption (up to $14,875) may protect more personal property, while Kentucky state exemptions may offer stronger homestead protection.

Homestead Exemption

Protects equity in your primary residence. Amounts vary by state. Check Kentucky homestead exemption amounts.

Vehicle Exemption

Protects equity in your car. Most states protect $2,000 to $7,500. See vehicle exemption details.

Retirement Accounts

401(k)s, IRAs, and pensions are generally fully exempt in bankruptcy. See retirement exemptions.

Wildcard Exemption

Applies to any property. Not all states offer this. See wildcard exemptions.

Kentucky Means Test Income Limits

If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 of the means test and likely qualify for Chapter 7.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$58,632$4,886

2 persons$76,222$6,352

3 persons$89,121$7,427

4 persons$103,779$8,648

5 persons$114,879$9,573

6 persons$125,979$10,498

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Kentucky Bankruptcy Statistics

Data from the Federal Judicial Center (FJC), covering 1,692 consumer bankruptcy cases filed in Kentucky.

ChapterCases FiledDischarge RateDismissal Rate

Chapter 731897.5%1.7%

Chapter 131,37472.1%27.0%

Chapter 7 Share

18.8% of consumer filings

Chapter 13 Share

81.2% of consumer filings

Discharge and dismissal rates reflect resolved cases only. Pending cases are excluded. Source: FJC Integrated Database.

Filing Bankruptcy in Kentucky

Districts

Kentucky has 2 federal bankruptcy districts. The district where you file depends on where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. Each district may have different local rules, no-look fee amounts, and trustee practices.

Filing Costs

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338. Chapter 13: $313. Fee waivers are available for filers below 150% of the poverty line. Installment payments available for all filers. Full cost breakdown.

Before You File

Complete credit counseling from an approved provider (required within 180 days before filing).

Determine your chapter -- Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 comparison.

Gather income documentation for the last 6 full calendar months.

List all debts, assets, income, and expenses for the bankruptcy schedules.

Research Kentucky exemptions to understand what property you can keep.

After You File

The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and lawsuits.

Attend the 341 meeting of creditors (usually 30-45 days after filing).

Complete a debtor education course (required before discharge).

Receive your discharge (Chapter 7: ~60-90 days after 341 meeting; Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan).

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Kentucky?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees in Kentucky typically range from $1,000 to $3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $5,000 for Chapter 13. You can also file without an attorney (pro se) and request to pay the filing fee in installments.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Kentucky?

The means test compares your household income to Kentucky's median. For a single person, the current threshold is approximately $58,632 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $103,779. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Can I use federal exemptions in Kentucky?

Yes. Kentucky allows you to choose between state exemptions and federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d). You must pick one complete set -- you cannot mix individual exemptions from both. Compare homestead, vehicle, and wildcard amounts under each system before deciding.

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit in Kentucky?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, many people begin rebuilding credit within 1-2 years of discharge and can qualify for a mortgage within 2-4 years.

Can I file bankruptcy again in Kentucky?

Yes, but there are waiting periods between discharges. Chapter 7 to Chapter 7: 8 years. Chapter 7 to Chapter 13: 4 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 13: 2 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 7: 6 years (unless you paid 100% or 70%+ with good faith). Use the free discharge eligibility screener to check your dates.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within Kentucky?

Kentucky has 2 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district. Check local court websites for county-to-district lookups.

What debts cannot be discharged in bankruptcy?

Most debts are dischargeable, but exceptions include most student loans (unless you prove undue hardship), child support and alimony, recent tax debts (generally last 3 years), debts from fraud or willful injury, DUI-related debts, and criminal fines. See nondischargeable.org for the full list.

## Bankruptcy in Maine: Filing Guide, Exemptions, and Statistics [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcyexemptionsbystate.com/states/maine.html

Exemptions by State | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Bankruptcy in Maine

Complete guide: exemptions, means test income limits, filing costs, and real case outcome data for Maine.

Important: This page provides educational information about bankruptcy in Maine. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

1

Bankruptcy District

$67,692

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Maine Exemptions

State Exemptions Only

Maine requires bankruptcy filers to use state exemptions exclusively. Federal exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available. Research Maine's homestead, vehicle, personal property, and wildcard exemptions before filing.

Homestead Exemption

Protects equity in your primary residence. Amounts vary by state. Check Maine homestead exemption amounts.

Vehicle Exemption

Protects equity in your car. Most states protect $2,000 to $7,500. See vehicle exemption details.

Retirement Accounts

401(k)s, IRAs, and pensions are generally fully exempt in bankruptcy. See retirement exemptions.

Wildcard Exemption

Applies to any property. Not all states offer this. See wildcard exemptions.

Maine Means Test Income Limits

If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 of the means test and likely qualify for Chapter 7.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$67,692$5,641

2 persons$88,000$7,333

3 persons$102,892$8,574

4 persons$119,815$9,985

5 persons$130,915$10,910

6 persons$142,015$11,835

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Filing Bankruptcy in Maine

Districts

Maine has a single federal bankruptcy district. All cases are filed in this one court, which simplifies the process.

Filing Costs

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338. Chapter 13: $313. Fee waivers are available for filers below 150% of the poverty line. Installment payments available for all filers. Full cost breakdown.

Before You File

Complete credit counseling from an approved provider (required within 180 days before filing).

Determine your chapter -- Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 comparison.

Gather income documentation for the last 6 full calendar months.

List all debts, assets, income, and expenses for the bankruptcy schedules.

Research Maine exemptions to understand what property you can keep.

After You File

The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and lawsuits.

Attend the 341 meeting of creditors (usually 30-45 days after filing).

Complete a debtor education course (required before discharge).

Receive your discharge (Chapter 7: ~60-90 days after 341 meeting; Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan).

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Maine?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees in Maine typically range from $1,000 to $3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $5,000 for Chapter 13. You can also file without an attorney (pro se) and request to pay the filing fee in installments.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Maine?

The means test compares your household income to Maine's median. For a single person, the current threshold is approximately $67,692 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $119,815. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

What property can I keep in Maine bankruptcy?

Maine requires filers to use state exemptions only. Key exemptions to research include the homestead exemption (how much home equity you can protect), vehicle exemption, personal property exemption, and any wildcard exemption. Check Maine statutes for current amounts.

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit in Maine?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, many people begin rebuilding credit within 1-2 years of discharge and can qualify for a mortgage within 2-4 years.

Can I file bankruptcy again in Maine?

Yes, but there are waiting periods between discharges. Chapter 7 to Chapter 7: 8 years. Chapter 7 to Chapter 13: 4 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 13: 2 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 7: 6 years (unless you paid 100% or 70%+ with good faith). Use the free discharge eligibility screener to check your dates.

What debts cannot be discharged in bankruptcy?

Most debts are dischargeable, but exceptions include most student loans (unless you prove undue hardship), child support and alimony, recent tax debts (generally last 3 years), debts from fraud or willful injury, DUI-related debts, and criminal fines. See nondischargeable.org for the full list.

Bankruptcy in Nearby States

New Hampshire · Massachusetts

Free Bankruptcy Tools

From the Open Bankruptcy Project -- free information, no strings.

Discharge Screener
Means Test Calculator

Related Guides

Bankruptcy Exemptions Guide

Bankruptcy Lien Guide

Keep Your Car in Bankruptcy

Keep Your House in Bankruptcy

Part of the Open Bankruptcy Project | Free information, no strings.

Automatic Stay ·
341 Meeting ·
523(a) Exceptions ·
727(a)(8) ·
Ch 7 vs 13 ·
How to File ·
Cost Guide ·
Non-Dischargeable Debts

## Bankruptcy in Maryland: Filing Guide, Exemptions, and Statistics [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcyexemptionsbystate.com/states/maryland.html

Exemptions by State | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Bankruptcy in Maryland

Complete guide: exemptions, means test income limits, filing costs, and real case outcome data for Maryland.

Important: This page provides educational information about bankruptcy in Maryland. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

1

Bankruptcy District

$97,203

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Maryland Exemptions

State Exemptions Only

Maryland requires bankruptcy filers to use state exemptions exclusively. Federal exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available. Research Maryland's homestead, vehicle, personal property, and wildcard exemptions before filing.

Homestead Exemption

Protects equity in your primary residence. Amounts vary by state. Check Maryland homestead exemption amounts.

Vehicle Exemption

Protects equity in your car. Most states protect $2,000 to $7,500. See vehicle exemption details.

Retirement Accounts

401(k)s, IRAs, and pensions are generally fully exempt in bankruptcy. See retirement exemptions.

Wildcard Exemption

Applies to any property. Not all states offer this. See wildcard exemptions.

Maryland Means Test Income Limits

If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 of the means test and likely qualify for Chapter 7.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$97,203$8,100

2 persons$126,364$10,530

3 persons$147,749$12,312

4 persons$172,049$14,337

5 persons$183,149$15,262

6 persons$194,249$16,187

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Maryland Bankruptcy Statistics

Data from the Federal Judicial Center (FJC), covering 559 consumer bankruptcy cases filed in Maryland.

ChapterCases FiledDischarge RateDismissal Rate

Chapter 742097.4%0.0%

Chapter 13139Insufficient dataInsufficient data

Chapter 7 Share

75.1% of consumer filings

Chapter 13 Share

24.9% of consumer filings

Discharge and dismissal rates reflect resolved cases only. Pending cases are excluded. Source: FJC Integrated Database.

Filing Bankruptcy in Maryland

Districts

Maryland has a single federal bankruptcy district. All cases are filed in this one court, which simplifies the process.

Filing Costs

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338. Chapter 13: $313. Fee waivers are available for filers below 150% of the poverty line. Installment payments available for all filers. Full cost breakdown.

Before You File

Complete credit counseling from an approved provider (required within 180 days before filing).

Determine your chapter -- Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 comparison.

Gather income documentation for the last 6 full calendar months.

List all debts, assets, income, and expenses for the bankruptcy schedules.

Research Maryland exemptions to understand what property you can keep.

After You File

The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and lawsuits.

Attend the 341 meeting of creditors (usually 30-45 days after filing).

Complete a debtor education course (required before discharge).

Receive your discharge (Chapter 7: ~60-90 days after 341 meeting; Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan).

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Maryland?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees in Maryland typically range from $1,000 to $3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $5,000 for Chapter 13. You can also file without an attorney (pro se) and request to pay the filing fee in installments.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Maryland?

The means test compares your household income to Maryland's median. For a single person, the current threshold is approximately $97,203 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $172,049. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

What property can I keep in Maryland bankruptcy?

Maryland requires filers to use state exemptions only. Key exemptions to research include the homestead exemption (how much home equity you can protect), vehicle exemption, personal property exemption, and any wildcard exemption. Check Maryland statutes for current amounts.

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit in Maryland?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, many people begin rebuilding credit within 1-2 years of discharge and can qualify for a mortgage within 2-4 years.

Can I file bankruptcy again in Maryland?

Yes, but there are waiting periods between discharges. Chapter 7 to Chapter 7: 8 years. Chapter 7 to Chapter 13: 4 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 13: 2 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 7: 6 years (unless you paid 100% or 70%+ with good faith). Use the free discharge eligibility screener to check your dates.

What debts cannot be discharged in bankruptcy?

Most debts are dischargeable, but exceptions include most student loans (unless you prove undue hardship), child support and alimony, recent tax debts (generally last 3 years), debts from fraud or willful injury, DUI-related debts, and criminal fines. See nondischargeable.org for the full list.

## Bankruptcy in Massachusetts: Filing Guide, Exemptions, and Statistics [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcyexemptionsbystate.com/states/massachusetts.html

Exemptions by State | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Bankruptcy in Massachusetts

Complete guide: exemptions, means test income limits, filing costs, and real case outcome data for Massachusetts.

Important: This page provides educational information about bankruptcy in Massachusetts. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

1

Bankruptcy District

$95,727

Median Income (1 person)

Yes

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Massachusetts Exemptions

Federal Exemptions Available

Filers in Massachusetts can choose between state and federal bankruptcy exemptions. Compare both sets carefully -- the federal wildcard exemption (up to $14,875) may protect more personal property, while Massachusetts state exemptions may offer stronger homestead protection.

Homestead Exemption

Protects equity in your primary residence. Amounts vary by state. Check Massachusetts homestead exemption amounts.

Vehicle Exemption

Protects equity in your car. Most states protect $2,000 to $7,500. See vehicle exemption details.

Retirement Accounts

401(k)s, IRAs, and pensions are generally fully exempt in bankruptcy. See retirement exemptions.

Wildcard Exemption

Applies to any property. Not all states offer this. See wildcard exemptions.

Massachusetts Means Test Income Limits

If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 of the means test and likely qualify for Chapter 7.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$95,727$7,977

2 persons$124,445$10,370

3 persons$145,505$12,125

4 persons$169,437$14,120

5 persons$180,537$15,045

6 persons$191,637$15,970

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Massachusetts Bankruptcy Statistics

Data from the Federal Judicial Center (FJC), covering 1,356 consumer bankruptcy cases filed in Massachusetts.

ChapterCases FiledDischarge RateDismissal Rate

Chapter 71,29098.3%0.9%

Chapter 136659.1%40.9%

Chapter 7 Share

95.1% of consumer filings

Chapter 13 Share

4.9% of consumer filings

Discharge and dismissal rates reflect resolved cases only. Pending cases are excluded. Source: FJC Integrated Database.

Filing Bankruptcy in Massachusetts

Districts

Massachusetts has a single federal bankruptcy district. All cases are filed in this one court, which simplifies the process.

Filing Costs

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338. Chapter 13: $313. Fee waivers are available for filers below 150% of the poverty line. Installment payments available for all filers. Full cost breakdown.

Before You File

Complete credit counseling from an approved provider (required within 180 days before filing).

Determine your chapter -- Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 comparison.

Gather income documentation for the last 6 full calendar months.

List all debts, assets, income, and expenses for the bankruptcy schedules.

Research Massachusetts exemptions to understand what property you can keep.

After You File

The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and lawsuits.

Attend the 341 meeting of creditors (usually 30-45 days after filing).

Complete a debtor education course (required before discharge).

Receive your discharge (Chapter 7: ~60-90 days after 341 meeting; Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan).

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Massachusetts?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees in Massachusetts typically range from $1,000 to $3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $5,000 for Chapter 13. You can also file without an attorney (pro se) and request to pay the filing fee in installments.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Massachusetts?

The means test compares your household income to Massachusetts's median. For a single person, the current threshold is approximately $95,727 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $169,437. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Can I use federal exemptions in Massachusetts?

Yes. Massachusetts allows you to choose between state exemptions and federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d). You must pick one complete set -- you cannot mix individual exemptions from both. Compare homestead, vehicle, and wildcard amounts under each system before deciding.

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit in Massachusetts?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, many people begin rebuilding credit within 1-2 years of discharge and can qualify for a mortgage within 2-4 years.

Can I file bankruptcy again in Massachusetts?

Yes, but there are waiting periods between discharges. Chapter 7 to Chapter 7: 8 years. Chapter 7 to Chapter 13: 4 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 13: 2 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 7: 6 years (unless you paid 100% or 70%+ with good faith). Use the free discharge eligibility screener to check your dates.

What debts cannot be discharged in bankruptcy?

Most debts are dischargeable, but exceptions include most student loans (unless you prove undue hardship), child support and alimony, recent tax debts (generally last 3 years), debts from fraud or willful injury, DUI-related debts, and criminal fines. See nondischargeable.org for the full list.

## Bankruptcy in Michigan: Filing Guide, Exemptions, and Statistics [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcyexemptionsbystate.com/states/michigan.html

Exemptions by State | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Bankruptcy in Michigan

Complete guide: exemptions, means test income limits, filing costs, and real case outcome data for Michigan.

Important: This page provides educational information about bankruptcy in Michigan. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

2

Bankruptcy Districts

$66,087

Median Income (1 person)

Yes

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Michigan Exemptions

Federal Exemptions Available

Filers in Michigan can choose between state and federal bankruptcy exemptions. Compare both sets carefully -- the federal wildcard exemption (up to $14,875) may protect more personal property, while Michigan state exemptions may offer stronger homestead protection.

Homestead Exemption

Protects equity in your primary residence. Amounts vary by state. Check Michigan homestead exemption amounts.

Vehicle Exemption

Protects equity in your car. Most states protect $2,000 to $7,500. See vehicle exemption details.

Retirement Accounts

401(k)s, IRAs, and pensions are generally fully exempt in bankruptcy. See retirement exemptions.

Wildcard Exemption

Applies to any property. Not all states offer this. See wildcard exemptions.

Michigan Means Test Income Limits

If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 of the means test and likely qualify for Chapter 7.

2 persons$85,913$7,159

Michigan Bankruptcy Statistics

Data from the Federal Judicial Center (FJC), covering 1,133 consumer bankruptcy cases filed in Michigan.

ChapterCases FiledDischarge RateDismissal Rate

Chapter 7633100.0%0.0%

Chapter 1350048.3%51.0%

Chapter 7 Share

55.9% of consumer filings

Chapter 13 Share

44.1% of consumer filings

Discharge and dismissal rates reflect resolved cases only. Pending cases are excluded. Source: FJC Integrated Database.

Filing Bankruptcy in Michigan

Districts

Michigan has 2 federal bankruptcy districts. The district where you file depends on where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. Each district may have different local rules, no-look fee amounts, and trustee practices.

Filing Costs

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338. Chapter 13: $313. Fee waivers are available for filers below 150% of the poverty line. Installment payments available for all filers. Full cost breakdown.

Before You File

Complete credit counseling from an approved provider (required within 180 days before filing).

Determine your chapter -- Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 comparison.

Gather income documentation for the last 6 full calendar months.

List all debts, assets, income, and expenses for the bankruptcy schedules.

Research Michigan exemptions to understand what property you can keep.

After You File

The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and lawsuits.

Attend the 341 meeting of creditors (usually 30-45 days after filing).

Complete a debtor education course (required before discharge).

Receive your discharge (Chapter 7: ~60-90 days after 341 meeting; Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan).

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Michigan?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees in Michigan typically range from $1,000 to $3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $5,000 for Chapter 13. You can also file without an attorney (pro se) and request to pay the filing fee in installments.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Michigan?

The means test compares your household income to Michigan's median. For a single person, the current threshold is approximately $66,087 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $116,974. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Can I use federal exemptions in Michigan?

Yes. Michigan allows you to choose between state exemptions and federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d). You must pick one complete set -- you cannot mix individual exemptions from both. Compare homestead, vehicle, and wildcard amounts under each system before deciding.

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit in Michigan?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, many people begin rebuilding credit within 1-2 years of discharge and can qualify for a mortgage within 2-4 years.

Can I file bankruptcy again in Michigan?

Yes, but there are waiting periods between discharges. Chapter 7 to Chapter 7: 8 years. Chapter 7 to Chapter 13: 4 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 13: 2 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 7: 6 years (unless you paid 100% or 70%+ with good faith). Use the free discharge eligibility screener to check your dates.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within Michigan?

Michigan has 2 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district. Check local court websites for county-to-district lookups.

What debts cannot be discharged in bankruptcy?

Most debts are dischargeable, but exceptions include most student loans (unless you prove undue hardship), child support and alimony, recent tax debts (generally last 3 years), debts from fraud or willful injury, DUI-related debts, and criminal fines. See nondischargeable.org for the full list.

Bankruptcy in Minnesota

Complete guide: exemptions, means test income limits, filing costs, and real case outcome data for Minnesota.

Important: This page provides educational information about bankruptcy in Minnesota. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

1

Bankruptcy District

$79,161

Median Income (1 person)

Yes

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Minnesota Exemptions

Federal Exemptions Available

Filers in Minnesota can choose between state and federal bankruptcy exemptions. Compare both sets carefully -- the federal wildcard exemption (up to $14,875) may protect more personal property, while Minnesota state exemptions may offer stronger homestead protection.

Homestead Exemption

Protects equity in your primary residence. Amounts vary by state. Check Minnesota homestead exemption amounts.

Vehicle Exemption

Protects equity in your car. Most states protect $2,000 to $7,500. See vehicle exemption details.

Retirement Accounts

401(k)s, IRAs, and pensions are generally fully exempt in bankruptcy. See retirement exemptions.

Wildcard Exemption

Applies to any property. Not all states offer this. See wildcard exemptions.

If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 of the means test and likely qualify for Chapter 7.

2 persons$102,909$8,576

5 persons$151,215$12,601

Filing Bankruptcy in Minnesota

Districts

Minnesota has a single federal bankruptcy district. All cases are filed in this one court, which simplifies the process.

Filing Costs

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338. Chapter 13: $313. Fee waivers are available for filers below 150% of the poverty line. Installment payments available for all filers. Full cost breakdown.

Before You File

Complete credit counseling from an approved provider (required within 180 days before filing).

Determine your chapter -- Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 comparison.

Gather income documentation for the last 6 full calendar months.

List all debts, assets, income, and expenses for the bankruptcy schedules.

Research Minnesota exemptions to understand what property you can keep.

After You File

The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and lawsuits.

Attend the 341 meeting of creditors (usually 30-45 days after filing).

Complete a debtor education course (required before discharge).

Receive your discharge (Chapter 7: ~60-90 days after 341 meeting; Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan).

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Minnesota?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees in Minnesota typically range from $1,000 to $3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $5,000 for Chapter 13. You can also file without an attorney (pro se) and request to pay the filing fee in installments.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Minnesota?

The means test compares your household income to Minnesota's median. For a single person, the current threshold is approximately $79,161 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $140,115. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Can I use federal exemptions in Minnesota?

Yes. Minnesota allows you to choose between state exemptions and federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d). You must pick one complete set -- you cannot mix individual exemptions from both. Compare homestead, vehicle, and wildcard amounts under each system before deciding.

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit in Minnesota?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, many people begin rebuilding credit within 1-2 years of discharge and can qualify for a mortgage within 2-4 years.

Can I file bankruptcy again in Minnesota?

Yes, but there are waiting periods between discharges. Chapter 7 to Chapter 7: 8 years. Chapter 7 to Chapter 13: 4 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 13: 2 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 7: 6 years (unless you paid 100% or 70%+ with good faith). Use the free discharge eligibility screener to check your dates.

What debts cannot be discharged in bankruptcy?

Most debts are dischargeable, but exceptions include most student loans (unless you prove undue hardship), child support and alimony, recent tax debts (generally last 3 years), debts from fraud or willful injury, DUI-related debts, and criminal fines. See nondischargeable.org for the full list.

## Bankruptcy in Mississippi: Filing Guide, Exemptions, and Statistics [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcyexemptionsbystate.com/states/mississippi.html

Exemptions by State | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Bankruptcy in Mississippi

Complete guide: exemptions, means test income limits, filing costs, and real case outcome data for Mississippi.

Important: This page provides educational information about bankruptcy in Mississippi. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

2

Bankruptcy Districts

$50,895

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Mississippi Exemptions

State Exemptions Only

Mississippi requires bankruptcy filers to use state exemptions exclusively. Federal exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available. Research Mississippi's homestead, vehicle, personal property, and wildcard exemptions before filing.

Homestead Exemption

Protects equity in your primary residence. Amounts vary by state. Check Mississippi homestead exemption amounts.

Vehicle Exemption

Protects equity in your car. Most states protect $2,000 to $7,500. See vehicle exemption details.

Retirement Accounts

401(k)s, IRAs, and pensions are generally fully exempt in bankruptcy. See retirement exemptions.

Wildcard Exemption

Applies to any property. Not all states offer this. See wildcard exemptions.

Mississippi Means Test Income Limits

If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 of the means test and likely qualify for Chapter 7.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$50,895$4,241

2 persons$66,164$5,514

3 persons$77,360$6,447

4 persons$90,084$7,507

5 persons$101,184$8,432

6 persons$112,284$9,357

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Filing Bankruptcy in Mississippi

Districts

Mississippi has 2 federal bankruptcy districts. The district where you file depends on where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. Each district may have different local rules, no-look fee amounts, and trustee practices.

Filing Costs

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338. Chapter 13: $313. Fee waivers are available for filers below 150% of the poverty line. Installment payments available for all filers. Full cost breakdown.

Before You File

Complete credit counseling from an approved provider (required within 180 days before filing).

Determine your chapter -- Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 comparison.

Gather income documentation for the last 6 full calendar months.

List all debts, assets, income, and expenses for the bankruptcy schedules.

Research Mississippi exemptions to understand what property you can keep.

After You File

The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and lawsuits.

Attend the 341 meeting of creditors (usually 30-45 days after filing).

Complete a debtor education course (required before discharge).

Receive your discharge (Chapter 7: ~60-90 days after 341 meeting; Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan).

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Mississippi?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees in Mississippi typically range from $1,000 to $3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $5,000 for Chapter 13. You can also file without an attorney (pro se) and request to pay the filing fee in installments.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Mississippi?

The means test compares your household income to Mississippi's median. For a single person, the current threshold is approximately $50,895 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $90,084. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

What property can I keep in Mississippi bankruptcy?

Mississippi requires filers to use state exemptions only. Key exemptions to research include the homestead exemption (how much home equity you can protect), vehicle exemption, personal property exemption, and any wildcard exemption. Check Mississippi statutes for current amounts.

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit in Mississippi?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, many people begin rebuilding credit within 1-2 years of discharge and can qualify for a mortgage within 2-4 years.

Can I file bankruptcy again in Mississippi?

Yes, but there are waiting periods between discharges. Chapter 7 to Chapter 7: 8 years. Chapter 7 to Chapter 13: 4 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 13: 2 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 7: 6 years (unless you paid 100% or 70%+ with good faith). Use the free discharge eligibility screener to check your dates.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within Mississippi?

Mississippi has 2 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district. Check local court websites for county-to-district lookups.

What debts cannot be discharged in bankruptcy?

Most debts are dischargeable, but exceptions include most student loans (unless you prove undue hardship), child support and alimony, recent tax debts (generally last 3 years), debts from fraud or willful injury, DUI-related debts, and criminal fines. See nondischargeable.org for the full list.

Bankruptcy in Nearby States

Alabama · Tennessee · Arkansas · Louisiana

Free Bankruptcy Tools

From the Open Bankruptcy Project -- free information, no strings.

Discharge Screener
Means Test Calculator

Bankruptcy Lien Guide

727(a)(8) ·
Ch 7 vs 13 ·

## Bankruptcy in Missouri: Filing Guide, Exemptions, and Statistics [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcyexemptionsbystate.com/states/missouri.html

Exemptions by State | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Bankruptcy in Missouri

Complete guide: exemptions, means test income limits, filing costs, and real case outcome data for Missouri.

Important: This page provides educational information about bankruptcy in Missouri. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

2

Bankruptcy Districts

$64,972

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Missouri Exemptions

State Exemptions Only

Missouri requires bankruptcy filers to use state exemptions exclusively. Federal exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available. Research Missouri's homestead, vehicle, personal property, and wildcard exemptions before filing.

Homestead Exemption

Protects equity in your primary residence. Amounts vary by state. Check Missouri homestead exemption amounts.

Vehicle Exemption

Protects equity in your car. Most states protect $2,000 to $7,500. See vehicle exemption details.

Retirement Accounts

401(k)s, IRAs, and pensions are generally fully exempt in bankruptcy. See retirement exemptions.

Wildcard Exemption

Applies to any property. Not all states offer this. See wildcard exemptions.

Missouri Means Test Income Limits

If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 of the means test and likely qualify for Chapter 7.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$64,972$5,414

2 persons$84,464$7,039

3 persons$98,757$8,230

4 persons$115,000$9,583

5 persons$126,100$10,508

6 persons$137,200$11,433

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Missouri Bankruptcy Statistics

Data from the Federal Judicial Center (FJC), covering 45,771 consumer bankruptcy cases filed in Missouri.

ChapterCases FiledDischarge RateDismissal Rate

Chapter 722,13299.0%0.8%

Chapter 1323,63955.1%44.7%

Chapter 7 Share

48.4% of consumer filings

Chapter 13 Share

51.6% of consumer filings

Discharge and dismissal rates reflect resolved cases only. Pending cases are excluded. Source: FJC Integrated Database.

Filing Bankruptcy in Missouri

Districts

Missouri has 2 federal bankruptcy districts. The district where you file depends on where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. Each district may have different local rules, no-look fee amounts, and trustee practices.

Filing Costs

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338. Chapter 13: $313. Fee waivers are available for filers below 150% of the poverty line. Installment payments available for all filers. Full cost breakdown.

Before You File

Complete credit counseling from an approved provider (required within 180 days before filing).

Determine your chapter -- Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 comparison.

Gather income documentation for the last 6 full calendar months.

List all debts, assets, income, and expenses for the bankruptcy schedules.

Research Missouri exemptions to understand what property you can keep.

After You File

The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and lawsuits.

Attend the 341 meeting of creditors (usually 30-45 days after filing).

Complete a debtor education course (required before discharge).

Receive your discharge (Chapter 7: ~60-90 days after 341 meeting; Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan).

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Missouri?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees in Missouri typically range from $1,000 to $3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $5,000 for Chapter 13. You can also file without an attorney (pro se) and request to pay the filing fee in installments.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Missouri?

The means test compares your household income to Missouri's median. For a single person, the current threshold is approximately $64,972 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $115,000. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

What property can I keep in Missouri bankruptcy?

Missouri requires filers to use state exemptions only. Key exemptions to research include the homestead exemption (how much home equity you can protect), vehicle exemption, personal property exemption, and any wildcard exemption. Check Missouri statutes for current amounts.

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit in Missouri?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, many people begin rebuilding credit within 1-2 years of discharge and can qualify for a mortgage within 2-4 years.

Can I file bankruptcy again in Missouri?

Yes, but there are waiting periods between discharges. Chapter 7 to Chapter 7: 8 years. Chapter 7 to Chapter 13: 4 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 13: 2 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 7: 6 years (unless you paid 100% or 70%+ with good faith). Use the free discharge eligibility screener to check your dates.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within Missouri?

Missouri has 2 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district. Check local court websites for county-to-district lookups.

What debts cannot be discharged in bankruptcy?

Most debts are dischargeable, but exceptions include most student loans (unless you prove undue hardship), child support and alimony, recent tax debts (generally last 3 years), debts from fraud or willful injury, DUI-related debts, and criminal fines. See nondischargeable.org for the full list.

Montana Exemptions

State Exemptions Only

Montana requires bankruptcy filers to use state exemptions exclusively. Federal exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available. Research Montana's homestead, vehicle, personal property, and wildcard exemptions before filing.

Homestead Exemption

Protects equity in your primary residence. Amounts vary by state. Check Montana homestead exemption amounts.

Vehicle Exemption

Protects equity in your car. Most states protect $2,000 to $7,500. See vehicle exemption details.

Retirement Accounts

401(k)s, IRAs, and pensions are generally fully exempt in bankruptcy. See retirement exemptions.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Filing Bankruptcy in Montana

Districts

Montana has a single federal bankruptcy district. All cases are filed in this one court, which simplifies the process.

Filing Costs

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338. Chapter 13: $313. Fee waivers are available for filers below 150% of the poverty line. Installment payments available for all filers. Full cost breakdown.

Before You File

Complete credit counseling from an approved provider (required within 180 days before filing).

Determine your chapter -- Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 comparison.

Gather income documentation for the last 6 full calendar months.

List all debts, assets, income, and expenses for the bankruptcy schedules.

Research Montana exemptions to understand what property you can keep.

After You File

The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and lawsuits.

Attend the 341 meeting of creditors (usually 30-45 days after filing).

Complete a debtor education course (required before discharge).

Receive your discharge (Chapter 7: ~60-90 days after 341 meeting; Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan).

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Montana?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees in Montana typically range from $1,000 to $3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $5,000 for Chapter 13. You can also file without an attorney (pro se) and request to pay the filing fee in installments.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Montana?

The means test compares your household income to Montana's median. For a single person, the current threshold is approximately $66,501 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $117,707. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

What property can I keep in Montana bankruptcy?

Montana requires filers to use state exemptions only. Key exemptions to research include the homestead exemption (how much home equity you can protect), vehicle exemption, personal property exemption, and any wildcard exemption. Check Montana statutes for current amounts.

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit in Montana?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, many people begin rebuilding credit within 1-2 years of discharge and can qualify for a mortgage within 2-4 years.

Can I file bankruptcy again in Montana?

Yes, but there are waiting periods between discharges. Chapter 7 to Chapter 7: 8 years. Chapter 7 to Chapter 13: 4 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 13: 2 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 7: 6 years (unless you paid 100% or 70%+ with good faith). Use the free discharge eligibility screener to check your dates.

What debts cannot be discharged in bankruptcy?

Most debts are dischargeable, but exceptions include most student loans (unless you prove undue hardship), child support and alimony, recent tax debts (generally last 3 years), debts from fraud or willful injury, DUI-related debts, and criminal fines. See nondischargeable.org for the full list.

## Bankruptcy in New Hampshire: Filing Guide, Exemptions, and Statistics [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcyexemptionsbystate.com/states/new-hampshire.html

Exemptions by State | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Bankruptcy in New Hampshire

Complete guide: exemptions, means test income limits, filing costs, and real case outcome data for New Hampshire.

Important: This page provides educational information about bankruptcy in New Hampshire. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

1

Bankruptcy District

$86,997

Median Income (1 person)

Yes

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

New Hampshire Exemptions

Federal Exemptions Available

Filers in New Hampshire can choose between state and federal bankruptcy exemptions. Compare both sets carefully -- the federal wildcard exemption (up to $14,875) may protect more personal property, while New Hampshire state exemptions may offer stronger homestead protection.

Homestead Exemption

Protects equity in your primary residence. Amounts vary by state. Check New Hampshire homestead exemption amounts.

Vehicle Exemption

Protects equity in your car. Most states protect $2,000 to $7,500. See vehicle exemption details.

Retirement Accounts

401(k)s, IRAs, and pensions are generally fully exempt in bankruptcy. See retirement exemptions.

Wildcard Exemption

Applies to any property. Not all states offer this. See wildcard exemptions.

New Hampshire Means Test Income Limits

If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 of the means test and likely qualify for Chapter 7.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$86,997$7,250

2 persons$113,096$9,425

3 persons$132,235$11,020

4 persons$153,985$12,832

5 persons$165,085$13,757

6 persons$176,185$14,682

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Discharge Screener

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338. Chapter 13: $313. Fee waivers are available for filers below 150% of the poverty line. Installment payments available for all filers. Full cost breakdown.

Complete credit counseling from an approved provider (required within 180 days before filing).

Determine your chapter -- Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 comparison.

Gather income documentation for the last 6 full calendar months.

List all debts, assets, income, and expenses for the bankruptcy schedules.

Research New Hampshire exemptions to understand what property you can keep.

Attend the 341 meeting of creditors (usually 30-45 days after filing).

Complete a debtor education course (required before discharge).

Receive your discharge (Chapter 7: ~60-90 days after 341 meeting; Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan).

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees in New Hampshire typically range from $1,000 to $3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $5,000 for Chapter 13. You can also file without an attorney (pro se) and request to pay the filing fee in installments.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in New Hampshire?

The means test compares your household income to New Hampshire's median. For a single person, the current threshold is approximately $86,997 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $153,985. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Can I use federal exemptions in New Hampshire?

Yes. New Hampshire allows you to choose between state exemptions and federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d). You must pick one complete set -- you cannot mix individual exemptions from both. Compare homestead, vehicle, and wildcard amounts under each system before deciding.

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit in New Hampshire?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, many people begin rebuilding credit within 1-2 years of discharge and can qualify for a mortgage within 2-4 years.

Can I file bankruptcy again in New Hampshire?

Yes, but there are waiting periods between discharges. Chapter 7 to Chapter 7: 8 years. Chapter 7 to Chapter 13: 4 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 13: 2 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 7: 6 years (unless you paid 100% or 70%+ with good faith). Use the free discharge eligibility screener to check your dates.

What debts cannot be discharged in bankruptcy?

Most debts are dischargeable, but exceptions include most student loans (unless you prove undue hardship), child support and alimony, recent tax debts (generally last 3 years), debts from fraud or willful injury, DUI-related debts, and criminal fines. See nondischargeable.org for the full list.

Bankruptcy in Nearby States

Maine · Massachusetts · Vermont

From the Open Bankruptcy Project -- free information, no strings.

## Bankruptcy in New Jersey: Filing Guide, Exemptions, and Statistics [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcyexemptionsbystate.com/states/new-jersey.html

Exemptions by State | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Bankruptcy in New Jersey

Complete guide: exemptions, means test income limits, filing costs, and real case outcome data for New Jersey.

Important: This page provides educational information about bankruptcy in New Jersey. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

1

Bankruptcy District

$93,051

Median Income (1 person)

Yes

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

New Jersey Exemptions

Federal Exemptions Available

Filers in New Jersey can choose between state and federal bankruptcy exemptions. Compare both sets carefully -- the federal wildcard exemption (up to $14,875) may protect more personal property, while New Jersey state exemptions may offer stronger homestead protection.

Homestead Exemption

Protects equity in your primary residence. Amounts vary by state. Check New Jersey homestead exemption amounts.

Vehicle Exemption

Protects equity in your car. Most states protect $2,000 to $7,500. See vehicle exemption details.

Retirement Accounts

401(k)s, IRAs, and pensions are generally fully exempt in bankruptcy. See retirement exemptions.

Wildcard Exemption

Applies to any property. Not all states offer this. See wildcard exemptions.

New Jersey Means Test Income Limits

If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 of the means test and likely qualify for Chapter 7.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$93,051$7,754

2 persons$120,966$10,080

3 persons$141,438$11,786

4 persons$164,700$13,725

5 persons$175,800$14,650

6 persons$186,900$15,575

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

New Jersey Bankruptcy Statistics

Data from the Federal Judicial Center (FJC), covering 1,580 consumer bankruptcy cases filed in New Jersey.

ChapterCases FiledDischarge RateDismissal Rate

Chapter 752697.3%2.7%

Chapter 131,05431.0%68.8%

Chapter 7 Share

33.3% of consumer filings

Chapter 13 Share

66.7% of consumer filings

Discharge and dismissal rates reflect resolved cases only. Pending cases are excluded. Source: FJC Integrated Database.

Filing Bankruptcy in New Jersey

Districts

New Jersey has a single federal bankruptcy district. All cases are filed in this one court, which simplifies the process.

Filing Costs

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338. Chapter 13: $313. Fee waivers are available for filers below 150% of the poverty line. Installment payments available for all filers. Full cost breakdown.

Before You File

Complete credit counseling from an approved provider (required within 180 days before filing).

Determine your chapter -- Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 comparison.

Gather income documentation for the last 6 full calendar months.

List all debts, assets, income, and expenses for the bankruptcy schedules.

Research New Jersey exemptions to understand what property you can keep.

After You File

The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and lawsuits.

Attend the 341 meeting of creditors (usually 30-45 days after filing).

Complete a debtor education course (required before discharge).

Receive your discharge (Chapter 7: ~60-90 days after 341 meeting; Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan).

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in New Jersey?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees in New Jersey typically range from $1,000 to $3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $5,000 for Chapter 13. You can also file without an attorney (pro se) and request to pay the filing fee in installments.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in New Jersey?

The means test compares your household income to New Jersey's median. For a single person, the current threshold is approximately $93,051 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $164,700. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Can I use federal exemptions in New Jersey?

Yes. New Jersey allows you to choose between state exemptions and federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d). You must pick one complete set -- you cannot mix individual exemptions from both. Compare homestead, vehicle, and wildcard amounts under each system before deciding.

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit in New Jersey?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, many people begin rebuilding credit within 1-2 years of discharge and can qualify for a mortgage within 2-4 years.

Can I file bankruptcy again in New Jersey?

Yes, but there are waiting periods between discharges. Chapter 7 to Chapter 7: 8 years. Chapter 7 to Chapter 13: 4 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 13: 2 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 7: 6 years (unless you paid 100% or 70%+ with good faith). Use the free discharge eligibility screener to check your dates.

What debts cannot be discharged in bankruptcy?

Most debts are dischargeable, but exceptions include most student loans (unless you prove undue hardship), child support and alimony, recent tax debts (generally last 3 years), debts from fraud or willful injury, DUI-related debts, and criminal fines. See nondischargeable.org for the full list.

Protects equity in your primary residence. Amounts vary by state. Check New Mexico homestead exemption amounts.

Protects equity in your car. Most states protect $2,000 to $7,500. See vehicle exemption details.

Bankruptcy in New York

Complete guide: exemptions, means test income limits, filing costs, and real case outcome data for New York.

Important: This page provides educational information about bankruptcy in New York. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

4

Bankruptcy Districts

$73,272

Median Income (1 person)

Yes

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

New York Exemptions

Federal Exemptions Available

Filers in New York can choose between state and federal bankruptcy exemptions. Compare both sets carefully -- the federal wildcard exemption (up to $14,875) may protect more personal property, while New York state exemptions may offer stronger homestead protection.

Homestead Exemption

Protects equity in your primary residence. Amounts vary by state. Check New York homestead exemption amounts.

Vehicle Exemption

Protects equity in your car. Most states protect $2,000 to $7,500. See vehicle exemption details.

New York Bankruptcy Statistics

Data from the Federal Judicial Center (FJC), covering 1,231 consumer bankruptcy cases filed in New York.

ChapterCases FiledDischarge RateDismissal Rate

Chapter 71,14598.7%1.2%

Chapter 138628.2%71.8%

Chapter 7 Share

93.0% of consumer filings

Chapter 13 Share

7.0% of consumer filings

Discharge and dismissal rates reflect resolved cases only. Pending cases are excluded. Source: FJC Integrated Database.

Filing Bankruptcy in New York

Districts

New York has 4 federal bankruptcy districts. The district where you file depends on where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. Each district may have different local rules, no-look fee amounts, and trustee practices.

Filing Costs

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338. Chapter 13: $313. Fee waivers are available for filers below 150% of the poverty line. Installment payments available for all filers. Full cost breakdown.

Before You File

Complete credit counseling from an approved provider (required within 180 days before filing).

Determine your chapter -- Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 comparison.

Gather income documentation for the last 6 full calendar months.

List all debts, assets, income, and expenses for the bankruptcy schedules.

Research New York exemptions to understand what property you can keep.

After You File

The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and lawsuits.

Attend the 341 meeting of creditors (usually 30-45 days after filing).

Complete a debtor education course (required before discharge).

Receive your discharge (Chapter 7: ~60-90 days after 341 meeting; Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan).

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in New York?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees in New York typically range from $1,000 to $3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $5,000 for Chapter 13. You can also file without an attorney (pro se) and request to pay the filing fee in installments.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in New York?

The means test compares your household income to New York's median. For a single person, the current threshold is approximately $73,272 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $129,691. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Can I use federal exemptions in New York?

Yes. New York allows you to choose between state exemptions and federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d). You must pick one complete set -- you cannot mix individual exemptions from both. Compare homestead, vehicle, and wildcard amounts under each system before deciding.

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit in New York?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, many people begin rebuilding credit within 1-2 years of discharge and can qualify for a mortgage within 2-4 years.

Can I file bankruptcy again in New York?

Yes, but there are waiting periods between discharges. Chapter 7 to Chapter 7: 8 years. Chapter 7 to Chapter 13: 4 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 13: 2 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 7: 6 years (unless you paid 100% or 70%+ with good faith). Use the free discharge eligibility screener to check your dates.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within New York?

New York has 4 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district. Check local court websites for county-to-district lookups.

What debts cannot be discharged in bankruptcy?

Most debts are dischargeable, but exceptions include most student loans (unless you prove undue hardship), child support and alimony, recent tax debts (generally last 3 years), debts from fraud or willful injury, DUI-related debts, and criminal fines. See nondischargeable.org for the full list.

## Bankruptcy in North Carolina: Filing Guide, Exemptions, and Statistics [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcyexemptionsbystate.com/states/north-carolina.html

Exemptions by State | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Bankruptcy in North Carolina

Complete guide: exemptions, means test income limits, filing costs, and real case outcome data for North Carolina.

Important: This page provides educational information about bankruptcy in North Carolina. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

3

Bankruptcy Districts

$63,339

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

North Carolina Exemptions

State Exemptions Only

North Carolina requires bankruptcy filers to use state exemptions exclusively. Federal exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available. Research North Carolina's homestead, vehicle, personal property, and wildcard exemptions before filing.

Homestead Exemption

Protects equity in your primary residence. Amounts vary by state. Check North Carolina homestead exemption amounts.

Vehicle Exemption

Protects equity in your car. Most states protect $2,000 to $7,500. See vehicle exemption details.

Retirement Accounts

401(k)s, IRAs, and pensions are generally fully exempt in bankruptcy. See retirement exemptions.

Wildcard Exemption

Applies to any property. Not all states offer this. See wildcard exemptions.

North Carolina Means Test Income Limits

If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 of the means test and likely qualify for Chapter 7.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$63,339$5,278

2 persons$82,341$6,862

3 persons$96,275$8,023

4 persons$112,110$9,342

5 persons$123,210$10,268

6 persons$134,310$11,192

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Chapter 13554Insufficient dataInsufficient data

Chapter 7 Share

33.0% of consumer filings

Chapter 13 Share

67.0% of consumer filings

Discharge and dismissal rates reflect resolved cases only. Pending cases are excluded. Source: FJC Integrated Database.

Filing Bankruptcy in North Carolina

Districts

North Carolina has 3 federal bankruptcy districts. The district where you file depends on where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. Each district may have different local rules, no-look fee amounts, and trustee practices.

Filing Costs

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338. Chapter 13: $313. Fee waivers are available for filers below 150% of the poverty line. Installment payments available for all filers. Full cost breakdown.

Before You File

Complete credit counseling from an approved provider (required within 180 days before filing).

Determine your chapter -- Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 comparison.

Gather income documentation for the last 6 full calendar months.

List all debts, assets, income, and expenses for the bankruptcy schedules.

Research North Carolina exemptions to understand what property you can keep.

After You File

The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and lawsuits.

Attend the 341 meeting of creditors (usually 30-45 days after filing).

Complete a debtor education course (required before discharge).

Receive your discharge (Chapter 7: ~60-90 days after 341 meeting; Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan).

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in North Carolina?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees in North Carolina typically range from $1,000 to $3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $5,000 for Chapter 13. You can also file without an attorney (pro se) and request to pay the filing fee in installments.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in North Carolina?

The means test compares your household income to North Carolina's median. For a single person, the current threshold is approximately $63,339 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $112,110. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

What property can I keep in North Carolina bankruptcy?

North Carolina requires filers to use state exemptions only. Key exemptions to research include the homestead exemption (how much home equity you can protect), vehicle exemption, personal property exemption, and any wildcard exemption. Check North Carolina statutes for current amounts.

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit in North Carolina?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, many people begin rebuilding credit within 1-2 years of discharge and can qualify for a mortgage within 2-4 years.

Can I file bankruptcy again in North Carolina?

Yes, but there are waiting periods between discharges. Chapter 7 to Chapter 7: 8 years. Chapter 7 to Chapter 13: 4 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 13: 2 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 7: 6 years (unless you paid 100% or 70%+ with good faith). Use the free discharge eligibility screener to check your dates.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within North Carolina?

North Carolina has 3 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district. Check local court websites for county-to-district lookups.

What debts cannot be discharged in bankruptcy?

Most debts are dischargeable, but exceptions include most student loans (unless you prove undue hardship), child support and alimony, recent tax debts (generally last 3 years), debts from fraud or willful injury, DUI-related debts, and criminal fines. See nondischargeable.org for the full list.

Chapter 13 to Chapter 7: 6 years (unless you paid 100% or 70%+ with good faith). Use the free discharge eligibility screener to check your dates.

What debts cannot be discharged in bankruptcy?

Most debts are dischargeable, but exceptions include most student loans (unless you prove undue hardship), child support and alimony, recent tax debts (generally last 3 years), debts from fraud or willful injury, DUI-related debts, and criminal fines. See nondischargeable.org for the full list.

Bankruptcy in Nearby States

Minnesota · South Dakota · Montana
