## Bankruptcy in Ohio: Filing Guide, Exemptions, and Statistics [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcyexemptionsbystate.com/states/ohio.html

Exemptions by State | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Bankruptcy in Ohio

Complete guide: exemptions, means test income limits, filing costs, and real case outcome data for Ohio.

Important: This page provides educational information about bankruptcy in Ohio. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

2

Bankruptcy Districts

$63,564

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Ohio Exemptions

State Exemptions Only

Ohio requires bankruptcy filers to use state exemptions exclusively. Federal exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available. Research Ohio's homestead, vehicle, personal property, and wildcard exemptions before filing.

Homestead Exemption

Protects equity in your primary residence. Amounts vary by state. Check Ohio homestead exemption amounts.

Vehicle Exemption

Protects equity in your car. Most states protect $2,000 to $7,500. See vehicle exemption details.

Retirement Accounts

401(k)s, IRAs, and pensions are generally fully exempt in bankruptcy. See retirement exemptions.

Wildcard Exemption

Applies to any property. Not all states offer this. See wildcard exemptions.

Ohio Means Test Income Limits

If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 of the means test and likely qualify for Chapter 7.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$63,564$5,297

2 persons$82,633$6,886

3 persons$96,617$8,051

4 persons$112,508$9,376

5 persons$123,608$10,301

6 persons$134,708$11,226

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Ohio Bankruptcy Statistics

Data from the Federal Judicial Center (FJC), covering 3,971 consumer bankruptcy cases filed in Ohio.

ChapterCases FiledDischarge RateDismissal Rate

Chapter 73,53597.7%1.8%

Chapter 1343651.1%48.9%

Chapter 7 Share

89.0% of consumer filings

Chapter 13 Share

11.0% of consumer filings

Discharge and dismissal rates reflect resolved cases only. Pending cases are excluded. Source: FJC Integrated Database.

Filing Bankruptcy in Ohio

Districts

Ohio has 2 federal bankruptcy districts. The district where you file depends on where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. Each district may have different local rules, no-look fee amounts, and trustee practices.

Filing Costs

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338. Chapter 13: $313. Fee waivers are available for filers below 150% of the poverty line. Installment payments available for all filers. Full cost breakdown.

Before You File

Complete credit counseling from an approved provider (required within 180 days before filing).

Determine your chapter -- Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 comparison.

Gather income documentation for the last 6 full calendar months.

List all debts, assets, income, and expenses for the bankruptcy schedules.

Research Ohio exemptions to understand what property you can keep.

After You File

The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and lawsuits.

Attend the 341 meeting of creditors (usually 30-45 days after filing).

Complete a debtor education course (required before discharge).

Receive your discharge (Chapter 7: ~60-90 days after 341 meeting; Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan).

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Ohio?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees in Ohio typically range from $1,000 to $3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $5,000 for Chapter 13. You can also file without an attorney (pro se) and request to pay the filing fee in installments.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Ohio?

The means test compares your household income to Ohio's median. For a single person, the current threshold is approximately $63,564 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $112,508. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

What property can I keep in Ohio bankruptcy?

Ohio requires filers to use state exemptions only. Key exemptions to research include the homestead exemption (how much home equity you can protect), vehicle exemption, personal property exemption, and any wildcard exemption. Check Ohio statutes for current amounts.

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit in Ohio?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, many people begin rebuilding credit within 1-2 years of discharge and can qualify for a mortgage within 2-4 years.

Can I file bankruptcy again in Ohio?

Yes, but there are waiting periods between discharges. Chapter 7 to Chapter 7: 8 years. Chapter 7 to Chapter 13: 4 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 13: 2 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 7: 6 years (unless you paid 100% or 70%+ with good faith). Use the free discharge eligibility screener to check your dates.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within Ohio?

Ohio has 2 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district. Check local court websites for county-to-district lookups.

What debts cannot be discharged in bankruptcy?

Most debts are dischargeable, but exceptions include most student loans (unless you prove undue hardship), child support and alimony, recent tax debts (generally last 3 years), debts from fraud or willful injury, DUI-related debts, and criminal fines. See nondischargeable.org for the full list.

Bankruptcy in Nearby States

Michigan · Indiana · Kentucky · West Virginia

From the Open Bankruptcy Project -- free information, no strings.

Median Income (1 person)

Federal Exemptions

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Oklahoma Exemptions

State Exemptions Only

Oklahoma requires bankruptcy filers to use state exemptions exclusively. Federal exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available. Research Oklahoma's homestead, vehicle, personal property, and wildcard exemptions before filing.

Homestead Exemption

Protects equity in your primary residence. Amounts vary by state. Check Oklahoma homestead exemption amounts.

Vehicle Exemption

Protects equity in your car. Most states protect $2,000 to $7,500. See vehicle exemption details.

Retirement Accounts

401(k)s, IRAs, and pensions are generally fully exempt in bankruptcy. See retirement exemptions.

Wildcard Exemption

Applies to any property. Not all states offer this. See wildcard exemptions.

Filing Bankruptcy in Oklahoma

Districts

Oklahoma has 3 federal bankruptcy districts. The district where you file depends on where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. Each district may have different local rules, no-look fee amounts, and trustee practices.

Filing Costs

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338. Chapter 13: $313. Fee waivers are available for filers below 150% of the poverty line. Installment payments available for all filers. Full cost breakdown.

Before You File

Complete credit counseling from an approved provider (required within 180 days before filing).

Determine your chapter -- Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 comparison.

Gather income documentation for the last 6 full calendar months.

List all debts, assets, income, and expenses for the bankruptcy schedules.

Research Oklahoma exemptions to understand what property you can keep.

After You File

The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and lawsuits.

Attend the 341 meeting of creditors (usually 30-45 days after filing).

Complete a debtor education course (required before discharge).

Receive your discharge (Chapter 7: ~60-90 days after 341 meeting; Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan).

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Oklahoma?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees in Oklahoma typically range from $1,000 to $3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $5,000 for Chapter 13. You can also file without an attorney (pro se) and request to pay the filing fee in installments.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Oklahoma?

The means test compares your household income to Oklahoma's median. For a single person, the current threshold is approximately $60,636 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $107,326. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

What property can I keep in Oklahoma bankruptcy?

Oklahoma requires filers to use state exemptions only. Key exemptions to research include the homestead exemption (how much home equity you can protect), vehicle exemption, personal property exemption, and any wildcard exemption. Check Oklahoma statutes for current amounts.

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit in Oklahoma?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, many people begin rebuilding credit within 1-2 years of discharge and can qualify for a mortgage within 2-4 years.

Can I file bankruptcy again in Oklahoma?

Yes, but there are waiting periods between discharges. Chapter 7 to Chapter 7: 8 years. Chapter 7 to Chapter 13: 4 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 13: 2 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 7: 6 years (unless you paid 100% or 70%+ with good faith). Use the free discharge eligibility screener to check your dates.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within Oklahoma?

Oklahoma has 3 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district. Check local court websites for county-to-district lookups.

What debts cannot be discharged in bankruptcy?

Most debts are dischargeable, but exceptions include most student loans (unless you prove undue hardship), child support and alimony, recent tax debts (generally last 3 years), debts from fraud or willful injury, DUI-related debts, and criminal fines. See nondischargeable.org for the full list.

## Bankruptcy in Oregon: Filing Guide, Exemptions, and Statistics [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcyexemptionsbystate.com/states/oregon.html

Exemptions by State | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Bankruptcy in Oregon

Complete guide: exemptions, means test income limits, filing costs, and real case outcome data for Oregon.

Important: This page provides educational information about bankruptcy in Oregon. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

1

Bankruptcy District

$73,347

Median Income (1 person)

Yes

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Oregon Exemptions

Federal Exemptions Available

Filers in Oregon can choose between state and federal bankruptcy exemptions. Compare both sets carefully -- the federal wildcard exemption (up to $14,875) may protect more personal property, while Oregon state exemptions may offer stronger homestead protection.

Homestead Exemption

Protects equity in your primary residence. Amounts vary by state. Check Oregon homestead exemption amounts.

Vehicle Exemption

Protects equity in your car. Most states protect $2,000 to $7,500. See vehicle exemption details.

Retirement Accounts

401(k)s, IRAs, and pensions are generally fully exempt in bankruptcy. See retirement exemptions.

Wildcard Exemption

Applies to any property. Not all states offer this. See wildcard exemptions.

Oregon Means Test Income Limits

If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 of the means test and likely qualify for Chapter 7.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$73,347$6,112

2 persons$95,351$7,946

3 persons$111,487$9,291

4 persons$129,824$10,819

5 persons$140,924$11,744

6 persons$152,024$12,669

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Filing Bankruptcy in Oregon

Districts

Oregon has a single federal bankruptcy district. All cases are filed in this one court, which simplifies the process.

Filing Costs

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338. Chapter 13: $313. Fee waivers are available for filers below 150% of the poverty line. Installment payments available for all filers. Full cost breakdown.

Before You File

Complete credit counseling from an approved provider (required within 180 days before filing).

Determine your chapter -- Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 comparison.

Gather income documentation for the last 6 full calendar months.

List all debts, assets, income, and expenses for the bankruptcy schedules.

Research Oregon exemptions to understand what property you can keep.

After You File

The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and lawsuits.

Attend the 341 meeting of creditors (usually 30-45 days after filing).

Complete a debtor education course (required before discharge).

Receive your discharge (Chapter 7: ~60-90 days after 341 meeting; Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan).

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Oregon?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees in Oregon typically range from $1,000 to $3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $5,000 for Chapter 13. You can also file without an attorney (pro se) and request to pay the filing fee in installments.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Oregon?

The means test compares your household income to Oregon's median. For a single person, the current threshold is approximately $73,347 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $129,824. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Can I use federal exemptions in Oregon?

Yes. Oregon allows you to choose between state exemptions and federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d). You must pick one complete set -- you cannot mix individual exemptions from both. Compare homestead, vehicle, and wildcard amounts under each system before deciding.

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit in Oregon?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, many people begin rebuilding credit within 1-2 years of discharge and can qualify for a mortgage within 2-4 years.

Can I file bankruptcy again in Oregon?

Yes, but there are waiting periods between discharges. Chapter 7 to Chapter 7: 8 years. Chapter 7 to Chapter 13: 4 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 13: 2 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 7: 6 years (unless you paid 100% or 70%+ with good faith). Use the free discharge eligibility screener to check your dates.

What debts cannot be discharged in bankruptcy?

Most debts are dischargeable, but exceptions include most student loans (unless you prove undue hardship), child support and alimony, recent tax debts (generally last 3 years), debts from fraud or willful injury, DUI-related debts, and criminal fines. See nondischargeable.org for the full list.

## Bankruptcy in South Carolina: Filing Guide, Exemptions, and Statistics [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcyexemptionsbystate.com/states/south-carolina.html

Exemptions by State | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Bankruptcy in South Carolina

Complete guide: exemptions, means test income limits, filing costs, and real case outcome data for South Carolina.

Important: This page provides educational information about bankruptcy in South Carolina. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

1

Bankruptcy District

$61,050

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

South Carolina Exemptions

State Exemptions Only

South Carolina requires bankruptcy filers to use state exemptions exclusively. Federal exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available. Research South Carolina's homestead, vehicle, personal property, and wildcard exemptions before filing.

Homestead Exemption

Protects equity in your primary residence. Amounts vary by state. Check South Carolina homestead exemption amounts.

Vehicle Exemption

Protects equity in your car. Most states protect $2,000 to $7,500. See vehicle exemption details.

Retirement Accounts

401(k)s, IRAs, and pensions are generally fully exempt in bankruptcy. See retirement exemptions.

Wildcard Exemption

Applies to any property. Not all states offer this. See wildcard exemptions.

South Carolina Means Test Income Limits

If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 of the means test and likely qualify for Chapter 7.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$61,050$5,088

2 persons$79,365$6,614

3 persons$92,796$7,733

4 persons$108,058$9,005

5 persons$119,158$9,930

6 persons$130,258$10,855

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

South Carolina Bankruptcy Statistics

Data from the Federal Judicial Center (FJC), covering 635 consumer bankruptcy cases filed in South Carolina.

ChapterCases FiledDischarge RateDismissal Rate

Chapter 7301Insufficient dataInsufficient data

Chapter 13334Insufficient dataInsufficient data

Chapter 7 Share

47.4% of consumer filings

Chapter 13 Share

52.6% of consumer filings

Discharge and dismissal rates reflect resolved cases only. Pending cases are excluded. Source: FJC Integrated Database.

Filing Bankruptcy in South Carolina

Districts

South Carolina has a single federal bankruptcy district. All cases are filed in this one court, which simplifies the process.

Filing Costs

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338. Chapter 13: $313. Fee waivers are available for filers below 150% of the poverty line. Installment payments available for all filers. Full cost breakdown.

Before You File

Complete credit counseling from an approved provider (required within 180 days before filing).

Determine your chapter -- Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 comparison.

Gather income documentation for the last 6 full calendar months.

List all debts, assets, income, and expenses for the bankruptcy schedules.

Research South Carolina exemptions to understand what property you can keep.

After You File

The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and lawsuits.

Attend the 341 meeting of creditors (usually 30-45 days after filing).

Complete a debtor education course (required before discharge).

Receive your discharge (Chapter 7: ~60-90 days after 341 meeting; Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan).

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in South Carolina?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees in South Carolina typically range from $1,000 to $3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $5,000 for Chapter 13. You can also file without an attorney (pro se) and request to pay the filing fee in installments.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in South Carolina?

The means test compares your household income to South Carolina's median. For a single person, the current threshold is approximately $61,050 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $108,058. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

What property can I keep in South Carolina bankruptcy?

South Carolina requires filers to use state exemptions only. Key exemptions to research include the homestead exemption (how much home equity you can protect), vehicle exemption, personal property exemption, and any wildcard exemption. Check South Carolina statutes for current amounts.

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit in South Carolina?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, many people begin rebuilding credit within 1-2 years of discharge and can qualify for a mortgage within 2-4 years.

Can I file bankruptcy again in South Carolina?

Yes, but there are waiting periods between discharges. Chapter 7 to Chapter 7: 8 years. Chapter 7 to Chapter 13: 4 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 13: 2 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 7: 6 years (unless you paid 100% or 70%+ with good faith). Use the free discharge eligibility screener to check your dates.

What debts cannot be discharged in bankruptcy?

Most debts are dischargeable, but exceptions include most student loans (unless you prove undue hardship), child support and alimony, recent tax debts (generally last 3 years), debts from fraud or willful injury, DUI-related debts, and criminal fines. See nondischargeable.org for the full list.

Bankruptcy in Nearby States

North Carolina · Georgia

Free Bankruptcy Tools

From the Open Bankruptcy Project -- free information, no strings.

## Bankruptcy in South Dakota: Filing Guide, Exemptions, and Statistics [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcyexemptionsbystate.com/states/south-dakota.html

Exemptions by State | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Bankruptcy in South Dakota

Complete guide: exemptions, means test income limits, filing costs, and real case outcome data for South Dakota.

Important: This page provides educational information about bankruptcy in South Dakota. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

1

Bankruptcy District

$68,208

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

South Dakota Exemptions

State Exemptions Only

South Dakota requires bankruptcy filers to use state exemptions exclusively. Federal exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available. Research South Dakota's homestead, vehicle, personal property, and wildcard exemptions before filing.

Homestead Exemption

Protects equity in your primary residence. Amounts vary by state. Check South Dakota homestead exemption amounts.

Vehicle Exemption

Protects equity in your car. Most states protect $2,000 to $7,500. See vehicle exemption details.

Retirement Accounts

401(k)s, IRAs, and pensions are generally fully exempt in bankruptcy. See retirement exemptions.

Wildcard Exemption

Applies to any property. Not all states offer this. See wildcard exemptions.

South Dakota Means Test Income Limits

If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 of the means test and likely qualify for Chapter 7.

2 persons$88,670$7,389

3 persons$103,676$8,640

4 persons$120,728$10,061

5 persons$131,828$10,986

6 persons$142,928$11,911

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Filing Bankruptcy in South Dakota

Districts

South Dakota has a single federal bankruptcy district. All cases are filed in this one court, which simplifies the process.

Filing Costs

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338. Chapter 13: $313. Fee waivers are available for filers below 150% of the poverty line. Installment payments available for all filers. Full cost breakdown.

Before You File

Complete credit counseling from an approved provider (required within 180 days before filing).

Determine your chapter -- Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 comparison.

Gather income documentation for the last 6 full calendar months.

List all debts, assets, income, and expenses for the bankruptcy schedules.

Research South Dakota exemptions to understand what property you can keep.

After You File

The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and lawsuits.

Attend the 341 meeting of creditors (usually 30-45 days after filing).

Complete a debtor education course (required before discharge).

Receive your discharge (Chapter 7: ~60-90 days after 341 meeting; Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan).

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in South Dakota?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees in South Dakota typically range from $1,000 to $3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $5,000 for Chapter 13. You can also file without an attorney (pro se) and request to pay the filing fee in installments.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in South Dakota?

The means test compares your household income to South Dakota's median. For a single person, the current threshold is approximately $68,208 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $120,728. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

What property can I keep in South Dakota bankruptcy?

South Dakota requires filers to use state exemptions only. Key exemptions to research include the homestead exemption (how much home equity you can protect), vehicle exemption, personal property exemption, and any wildcard exemption. Check South Dakota statutes for current amounts.

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit in South Dakota?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, many people begin rebuilding credit within 1-2 years of discharge and can qualify for a mortgage within 2-4 years.

Can I file bankruptcy again in South Dakota?

Yes, but there are waiting periods between discharges. Chapter 7 to Chapter 7: 8 years. Chapter 7 to Chapter 13: 4 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 13: 2 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 7: 6 years (unless you paid 100% or 70%+ with good faith). Use the free discharge eligibility screener to check your dates.

What debts cannot be discharged in bankruptcy?

Most debts are dischargeable, but exceptions include most student loans (unless you prove undue hardship), child support and alimony, recent tax debts (generally last 3 years), debts from fraud or willful injury, DUI-related debts, and criminal fines. See nondischargeable.org for the full list.

## Bankruptcy in Tennessee: Filing Guide, Exemptions, and Statistics [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcyexemptionsbystate.com/states/tennessee.html

Exemptions by State | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Bankruptcy in Tennessee

Complete guide: exemptions, means test income limits, filing costs, and real case outcome data for Tennessee.

Important: This page provides educational information about bankruptcy in Tennessee. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

3

Bankruptcy Districts

$61,602

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Tennessee Exemptions

State Exemptions Only

Tennessee requires bankruptcy filers to use state exemptions exclusively. Federal exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available. Research Tennessee's homestead, vehicle, personal property, and wildcard exemptions before filing.

Homestead Exemption

Protects equity in your primary residence. Amounts vary by state. Check Tennessee homestead exemption amounts.

Vehicle Exemption

Protects equity in your car. Most states protect $2,000 to $7,500. See vehicle exemption details.

Retirement Accounts

401(k)s, IRAs, and pensions are generally fully exempt in bankruptcy. See retirement exemptions.

Wildcard Exemption

Applies to any property. Not all states offer this. See wildcard exemptions.

Tennessee Means Test Income Limits

If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 of the means test and likely qualify for Chapter 7.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Tennessee Bankruptcy Statistics

Data from the Federal Judicial Center (FJC), covering 1,840 consumer bankruptcy cases filed in Tennessee.

ChapterCases FiledDischarge RateDismissal Rate

Chapter 758995.5%4.5%

Chapter 131,25117.3%82.7%

Chapter 7 Share

32.0% of consumer filings

Chapter 13 Share

68.0% of consumer filings

Discharge and dismissal rates reflect resolved cases only. Pending cases are excluded. Source: FJC Integrated Database.

Filing Bankruptcy in Tennessee

Districts

Tennessee has 3 federal bankruptcy districts. The district where you file depends on where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. Each district may have different local rules, no-look fee amounts, and trustee practices.

Filing Costs

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338. Chapter 13: $313. Fee waivers are available for filers below 150% of the poverty line. Installment payments available for all filers. Full cost breakdown.

Before You File

Complete credit counseling from an approved provider (required within 180 days before filing).

Determine your chapter -- Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 comparison.

Gather income documentation for the last 6 full calendar months.

List all debts, assets, income, and expenses for the bankruptcy schedules.

Research Tennessee exemptions to understand what property you can keep.

After You File

The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and lawsuits.

Attend the 341 meeting of creditors (usually 30-45 days after filing).

Complete a debtor education course (required before discharge).

Receive your discharge (Chapter 7: ~60-90 days after 341 meeting; Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan).

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Tennessee?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees in Tennessee typically range from $1,000 to $3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $5,000 for Chapter 13. You can also file without an attorney (pro se) and request to pay the filing fee in installments.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Tennessee?

The means test compares your household income to Tennessee's median. For a single person, the current threshold is approximately $61,602 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $109,036. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

What property can I keep in Tennessee bankruptcy?

Tennessee requires filers to use state exemptions only. Key exemptions to research include the homestead exemption (how much home equity you can protect), vehicle exemption, personal property exemption, and any wildcard exemption. Check Tennessee statutes for current amounts.

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit in Tennessee?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, many people begin rebuilding credit within 1-2 years of discharge and can qualify for a mortgage within 2-4 years.

Can I file bankruptcy again in Tennessee?

Yes, but there are waiting periods between discharges. Chapter 7 to Chapter 7: 8 years. Chapter 7 to Chapter 13: 4 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 13: 2 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 7: 6 years (unless you paid 100% or 70%+ with good faith). Use the free discharge eligibility screener to check your dates.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within Tennessee?

Tennessee has 3 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district. Check local court websites for county-to-district lookups.

What debts cannot be discharged in bankruptcy?

Most debts are dischargeable, but exceptions include most student loans (unless you prove undue hardship), child support and alimony, recent tax debts (generally last 3 years), debts from fraud or willful injury, DUI-related debts, and criminal fines. See nondischargeable.org for the full list.

## Bankruptcy in Texas: Filing Guide, Exemptions, and Statistics [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcyexemptionsbystate.com/states/texas.html

Exemptions by State | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Bankruptcy in Texas

Complete guide: exemptions, means test income limits, filing costs, and real case outcome data for Texas.

Important: This page provides educational information about bankruptcy in Texas. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

4

Bankruptcy Districts

$66,837

Median Income (1 person)

Yes

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Texas Exemptions

Federal Exemptions Available

Filers in Texas can choose between state and federal bankruptcy exemptions. Compare both sets carefully -- the federal wildcard exemption (up to $14,875) may protect more personal property, while Texas state exemptions may offer stronger homestead protection.

Homestead Exemption

Protects equity in your primary residence. Amounts vary by state. Check Texas homestead exemption amounts.

Vehicle Exemption

Protects equity in your car. Most states protect $2,000 to $7,500. See vehicle exemption details.

Retirement Accounts

401(k)s, IRAs, and pensions are generally fully exempt in bankruptcy. See retirement exemptions.

Wildcard Exemption

Applies to any property. Not all states offer this. See wildcard exemptions.

Texas Means Test Income Limits

If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 of the means test and likely qualify for Chapter 7.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$66,837$5,570

2 persons$86,888$7,241

3 persons$101,592$8,466

4 persons$118,301$9,858

5 persons$129,401$10,783

6 persons$140,501$11,708

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Texas Bankruptcy Statistics

Data from the Federal Judicial Center (FJC), covering 30,781 consumer bankruptcy cases filed in Texas.

ChapterCases FiledDischarge RateDismissal Rate

Chapter 711,84497.8%2.0%

Chapter 1318,93736.8%63.2%

Chapter 7 Share

38.5% of consumer filings

Chapter 13 Share

61.5% of consumer filings

Discharge and dismissal rates reflect resolved cases only. Pending cases are excluded. Source: FJC Integrated Database.

Filing Bankruptcy in Texas

Districts

Texas has 4 federal bankruptcy districts. The district where you file depends on where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. Each district may have different local rules, no-look fee amounts, and trustee practices.

Filing Costs

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338. Chapter 13: $313. Fee waivers are available for filers below 150% of the poverty line. Installment payments available for all filers. Full cost breakdown.

Before You File

Complete credit counseling from an approved provider (required within 180 days before filing).

Determine your chapter -- Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 comparison.

Gather income documentation for the last 6 full calendar months.

List all debts, assets, income, and expenses for the bankruptcy schedules.

Research Texas exemptions to understand what property you can keep.

After You File

The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and lawsuits.

Attend the 341 meeting of creditors (usually 30-45 days after filing).

Complete a debtor education course (required before discharge).

Receive your discharge (Chapter 7: ~60-90 days after 341 meeting; Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan).

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Texas?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees in Texas typically range from $1,000 to $3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $5,000 for Chapter 13. You can also file without an attorney (pro se) and request to pay the filing fee in installments.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Texas?

The means test compares your household income to Texas's median. For a single person, the current threshold is approximately $66,837 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $118,301. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Can I use federal exemptions in Texas?

Yes. Texas allows you to choose between state exemptions and federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d). You must pick one complete set -- you cannot mix individual exemptions from both. Compare homestead, vehicle, and wildcard amounts under each system before deciding.

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit in Texas?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, many people begin rebuilding credit within 1-2 years of discharge and can qualify for a mortgage within 2-4 years.

Can I file bankruptcy again in Texas?

Yes, but there are waiting periods between discharges. Chapter 7 to Chapter 7: 8 years. Chapter 7 to Chapter 13: 4 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 13: 2 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 7: 6 years (unless you paid 100% or 70%+ with good faith). Use the free discharge eligibility screener to check your dates.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within Texas?

Texas has 4 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district. Check local court websites for county-to-district lookups.

What debts cannot be discharged in bankruptcy?

Most debts are dischargeable, but exceptions include most student loans (unless you prove undue hardship), child support and alimony, recent tax debts (generally last 3 years), debts from fraud or willful injury, DUI-related debts, and criminal fines. See nondischargeable.org for the full list.

Utah Exemptions

State Exemptions Only

Homestead Exemption

Protects equity in your primary residence. Amounts vary by state. Check Utah homestead exemption amounts.

Vehicle Exemption

Protects equity in your car. Most states protect $2,000 to $7,500. See vehicle exemption details.

Retirement Accounts

401(k)s, IRAs, and pensions are generally fully exempt in bankruptcy. See retirement exemptions.

Applies to any property. Not all states offer this. See wildcard exemptions.

Filing Bankruptcy in Utah

Districts

Utah has a single federal bankruptcy district. All cases are filed in this one court, which simplifies the process.

Filing Costs

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338. Chapter 13: $313. Fee waivers are available for filers below 150% of the poverty line. Installment payments available for all filers. Full cost breakdown.

Before You File

Complete credit counseling from an approved provider (required within 180 days before filing).

Determine your chapter -- Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 comparison.

Gather income documentation for the last 6 full calendar months.

List all debts, assets, income, and expenses for the bankruptcy schedules.

Research Utah exemptions to understand what property you can keep.

After You File

The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and lawsuits.

Attend the 341 meeting of creditors (usually 30-45 days after filing).

Complete a debtor education course (required before discharge).

Receive your discharge (Chapter 7: ~60-90 days after 341 meeting; Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan).

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Utah?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees in Utah typically range from $1,000 to $3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $5,000 for Chapter 13. You can also file without an attorney (pro se) and request to pay the filing fee in installments.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Utah?

The means test compares your household income to Utah's median. For a single person, the current threshold is approximately $73,044 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $129,288. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

What property can I keep in Utah bankruptcy?

Utah requires filers to use state exemptions only. Key exemptions to research include the homestead exemption (how much home equity you can protect), vehicle exemption, personal property exemption, and any wildcard exemption. Check Utah statutes for current amounts.

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit in Utah?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, many people begin rebuilding credit within 1-2 years of discharge and can qualify for a mortgage within 2-4 years.

Can I file bankruptcy again in Utah?

Yes, but there are waiting periods between discharges. Chapter 7 to Chapter 7: 8 years. Chapter 7 to Chapter 13: 4 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 13: 2 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 7: 6 years (unless you paid 100% or 70%+ with good faith). Use the free discharge eligibility screener to check your dates.

What debts cannot be discharged in bankruptcy?

Most debts are dischargeable, but exceptions include most student loans (unless you prove undue hardship), child support and alimony, recent tax debts (generally last 3 years), debts from fraud or willful injury, DUI-related debts, and criminal fines. See nondischargeable.org for the full list.

Vermont Exemptions

Homestead Exemption

Protects equity in your primary residence. Amounts vary by state. Check Vermont homestead exemption amounts.

Vehicle Exemption

Filing Bankruptcy in Vermont

Districts

Vermont has a single federal bankruptcy district. All cases are filed in this one court, which simplifies the process.

Filing Costs

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338. Chapter 13: $313. Fee waivers are available for filers below 150% of the poverty line. Installment payments available for all filers. Full cost breakdown.

Before You File

Complete credit counseling from an approved provider (required within 180 days before filing).

Determine your chapter -- Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 comparison.

Gather income documentation for the last 6 full calendar months.

List all debts, assets, income, and expenses for the bankruptcy schedules.

Research Vermont exemptions to understand what property you can keep.

After You File

The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and lawsuits.

Attend the 341 meeting of creditors (usually 30-45 days after filing).

Complete a debtor education course (required before discharge).

Receive your discharge (Chapter 7: ~60-90 days after 341 meeting; Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan).

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Vermont?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees in Vermont typically range from $1,000 to $3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $5,000 for Chapter 13. You can also file without an attorney (pro se) and request to pay the filing fee in installments.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Vermont?

The means test compares your household income to Vermont's median. For a single person, the current threshold is approximately $71,007 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $125,682. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Can I use federal exemptions in Vermont?

Yes. Vermont allows you to choose between state exemptions and federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d). You must pick one complete set -- you cannot mix individual exemptions from both. Compare homestead, vehicle, and wildcard amounts under each system before deciding.

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit in Vermont?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, many people begin rebuilding credit within 1-2 years of discharge and can qualify for a mortgage within 2-4 years.

Can I file bankruptcy again in Vermont?

Yes, but there are waiting periods between discharges. Chapter 7 to Chapter 7: 8 years. Chapter 7 to Chapter 13: 4 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 13: 2 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 7: 6 years (unless you paid 100% or 70%+ with good faith). Use the free discharge eligibility screener to check your dates.

What debts cannot be discharged in bankruptcy?

Most debts are dischargeable, but exceptions include most student loans (unless you prove undue hardship), child support and alimony, recent tax debts (generally last 3 years), debts from fraud or willful injury, DUI-related debts, and criminal fines. See nondischargeable.org for the full list.

Bankruptcy in Nearby States

New Hampshire · Massachusetts · New York

Free Bankruptcy Tools

From the Open Bankruptcy Project -- free information, no strings.

Discharge Screener
Means Test Calculator

Bankruptcy Lien Guide

## Bankruptcy in Virginia: Filing Guide, Exemptions, and Statistics [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcyexemptionsbystate.com/states/virginia.html

Exemptions by State | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Bankruptcy in Virginia

Complete guide: exemptions, means test income limits, filing costs, and real case outcome data for Virginia.

Important: This page provides educational information about bankruptcy in Virginia. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

2

Bankruptcy Districts

$84,396

Median Income (1 person)

No

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Virginia Exemptions

State Exemptions Only

Virginia requires bankruptcy filers to use state exemptions exclusively. Federal exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available. Research Virginia's homestead, vehicle, personal property, and wildcard exemptions before filing.

Homestead Exemption

Protects equity in your primary residence. Amounts vary by state. Check Virginia homestead exemption amounts.

Vehicle Exemption

Protects equity in your car. Most states protect $2,000 to $7,500. See vehicle exemption details.

Retirement Accounts

401(k)s, IRAs, and pensions are generally fully exempt in bankruptcy. See retirement exemptions.

Wildcard Exemption

Applies to any property. Not all states offer this. See wildcard exemptions.

Virginia Means Test Income Limits

If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 of the means test and likely qualify for Chapter 7.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$84,396$7,033

2 persons$109,715$9,143

3 persons$128,282$10,690

4 persons$149,381$12,448

5 persons$160,481$13,373

6 persons$171,581$14,298

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Virginia Bankruptcy Statistics

Data from the Federal Judicial Center (FJC), covering 1,064 consumer bankruptcy cases filed in Virginia.

ChapterCases FiledDischarge RateDismissal Rate

Chapter 7514Insufficient dataInsufficient data

Chapter 1355071.2%28.7%

Chapter 7 Share

48.3% of consumer filings

Chapter 13 Share

51.7% of consumer filings

Discharge and dismissal rates reflect resolved cases only. Pending cases are excluded. Source: FJC Integrated Database.

Filing Bankruptcy in Virginia

Districts

Virginia has 2 federal bankruptcy districts. The district where you file depends on where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. Each district may have different local rules, no-look fee amounts, and trustee practices.

Filing Costs

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338. Chapter 13: $313. Fee waivers are available for filers below 150% of the poverty line. Installment payments available for all filers. Full cost breakdown.

Before You File

Complete credit counseling from an approved provider (required within 180 days before filing).

Determine your chapter -- Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 comparison.

Gather income documentation for the last 6 full calendar months.

List all debts, assets, income, and expenses for the bankruptcy schedules.

Research Virginia exemptions to understand what property you can keep.

After You File

The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and lawsuits.

Attend the 341 meeting of creditors (usually 30-45 days after filing).

Complete a debtor education course (required before discharge).

Receive your discharge (Chapter 7: ~60-90 days after 341 meeting; Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan).

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Virginia?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees in Virginia typically range from $1,000 to $3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $5,000 for Chapter 13. You can also file without an attorney (pro se) and request to pay the filing fee in installments.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Virginia?

The means test compares your household income to Virginia's median. For a single person, the current threshold is approximately $84,396 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $149,381. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

What property can I keep in Virginia bankruptcy?

Virginia requires filers to use state exemptions only. Key exemptions to research include the homestead exemption (how much home equity you can protect), vehicle exemption, personal property exemption, and any wildcard exemption. Check Virginia statutes for current amounts.

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit in Virginia?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, many people begin rebuilding credit within 1-2 years of discharge and can qualify for a mortgage within 2-4 years.

Can I file bankruptcy again in Virginia?

Yes, but there are waiting periods between discharges. Chapter 7 to Chapter 7: 8 years. Chapter 7 to Chapter 13: 4 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 13: 2 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 7: 6 years (unless you paid 100% or 70%+ with good faith). Use the free discharge eligibility screener to check your dates.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within Virginia?

Virginia has 2 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district. Check local court websites for county-to-district lookups.

What debts cannot be discharged in bankruptcy?

Most debts are dischargeable, but exceptions include most student loans (unless you prove undue hardship), child support and alimony, recent tax debts (generally last 3 years), debts from fraud or willful injury, DUI-related debts, and criminal fines. See nondischargeable.org for the full list.

Maryland · West Virginia · Kentucky · Tennessee

## Bankruptcy in Washington: Filing Guide, Exemptions, and Statistics [2026]
Source: https://bankruptcyexemptionsbystate.com/states/washington.html

Exemptions by State | Means Test Calculator | Discharge Screener | Research

Bankruptcy in Washington

Complete guide: exemptions, means test income limits, filing costs, and real case outcome data for Washington.

Important: This page provides educational information about bankruptcy in Washington. It is not legal advice. Median income figures are approximate (Census/DOJ, effective April 2026). Verify current amounts at the DOJ Means Testing page.

2

Bankruptcy Districts

$85,446

Median Income (1 person)

Yes

Federal Exemptions

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Washington Exemptions

Federal Exemptions Available

Filers in Washington can choose between state and federal bankruptcy exemptions. Compare both sets carefully -- the federal wildcard exemption (up to $14,875) may protect more personal property, while Washington state exemptions may offer stronger homestead protection.

Homestead Exemption

Protects equity in your primary residence. Amounts vary by state. Check Washington homestead exemption amounts.

Vehicle Exemption

Protects equity in your car. Most states protect $2,000 to $7,500. See vehicle exemption details.

Retirement Accounts

401(k)s, IRAs, and pensions are generally fully exempt in bankruptcy. See retirement exemptions.

Wildcard Exemption

Applies to any property. Not all states offer this. See wildcard exemptions.

Washington Means Test Income Limits

If your household income is at or below these thresholds, you pass Part 1 of the means test and likely qualify for Chapter 7.

Household SizeAnnual ThresholdMonthly Equivalent

1 person$85,446$7,120

2 persons$111,080$9,257

3 persons$129,878$10,823

4 persons$151,239$12,603

5 persons$162,339$13,528

6 persons$173,439$14,453

For each additional person above 4, add approximately $11,100 to the 4-person figure.

Check Your Eligibility

Enter your income and household size for an instant result.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Filing Bankruptcy in Washington

Districts

Washington has 2 federal bankruptcy districts. The district where you file depends on where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. Each district may have different local rules, no-look fee amounts, and trustee practices.

Filing Costs

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338. Chapter 13: $313. Fee waivers are available for filers below 150% of the poverty line. Installment payments available for all filers. Full cost breakdown.

Before You File

Complete credit counseling from an approved provider (required within 180 days before filing).

Determine your chapter -- Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 comparison.

Gather income documentation for the last 6 full calendar months.

List all debts, assets, income, and expenses for the bankruptcy schedules.

Research Washington exemptions to understand what property you can keep.

After You File

The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and lawsuits.

Attend the 341 meeting of creditors (usually 30-45 days after filing).

Complete a debtor education course (required before discharge).

Receive your discharge (Chapter 7: ~60-90 days after 341 meeting; Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan).

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Washington?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees in Washington typically range from $1,000 to $3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $5,000 for Chapter 13. You can also file without an attorney (pro se) and request to pay the filing fee in installments.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Washington?

The means test compares your household income to Washington's median. For a single person, the current threshold is approximately $85,446 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $151,239. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

Can I use federal exemptions in Washington?

Yes. Washington allows you to choose between state exemptions and federal bankruptcy exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d). You must pick one complete set -- you cannot mix individual exemptions from both. Compare homestead, vehicle, and wildcard amounts under each system before deciding.

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit in Washington?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, many people begin rebuilding credit within 1-2 years of discharge and can qualify for a mortgage within 2-4 years.

Can I file bankruptcy again in Washington?

Yes, but there are waiting periods between discharges. Chapter 7 to Chapter 7: 8 years. Chapter 7 to Chapter 13: 4 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 13: 2 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 7: 6 years (unless you paid 100% or 70%+ with good faith). Use the free discharge eligibility screener to check your dates.

Which bankruptcy court do I file in within Washington?

Washington has 2 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district. Check local court websites for county-to-district lookups.

What debts cannot be discharged in bankruptcy?

Most debts are dischargeable, but exceptions include most student loans (unless you prove undue hardship), child support and alimony, recent tax debts (generally last 3 years), debts from fraud or willful injury, DUI-related debts, and criminal fines. See nondischargeable.org for the full list.

West Virginia Exemptions

State Exemptions Only

West Virginia requires bankruptcy filers to use state exemptions exclusively. Federal exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available. Research West Virginia's homestead, vehicle, personal property, and wildcard exemptions before filing.

Homestead Exemption

Protects equity in your primary residence. Amounts vary by state. Check West Virginia homestead exemption amounts.

Vehicle Exemption

Protects equity in your car. Most states protect $2,000 to $7,500. See vehicle exemption details.

Retirement Accounts

401(k)s, IRAs, and pensions are generally fully exempt in bankruptcy. See retirement exemptions.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in West Virginia?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees in West Virginia typically range from $1,000 to $3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $5,000 for Chapter 13. You can also file without an attorney (pro se) and request to pay the filing fee in installments.

Homestead Exemption

Protects equity in your primary residence. Amounts vary by state. Check Wisconsin homestead exemption amounts.

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338. Chapter 13: $313. Fee waivers are available for filers below 150% of the poverty line. Installment payments available for all filers. Full cost breakdown.

Complete a debtor education course (required before discharge).

Receive your discharge (Chapter 7: ~60-90 days after 341 meeting; Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan).

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Wisconsin?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees in Wisconsin typically range from $1,000 to $3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $5,000 for Chapter 13. You can also file without an attorney (pro se) and request to pay the filing fee in installments.

Wisconsin has 2 federal bankruptcy districts. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days. If you recently moved, the 180-day rule may require filing in your previous district. Check local court websites for county-to-district lookups.

Bankruptcy in Wyoming

Complete guide: exemptions, means test income limits, filing costs, and real case outcome data for Wyoming.

Bankruptcy District

$71,571

Median Income (1 person)

No

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

Wyoming Exemptions

State Exemptions Only

Wyoming requires bankruptcy filers to use state exemptions exclusively. Federal exemptions under 11 U.S.C. Section 522(d) are not available. Research Wyoming's homestead, vehicle, personal property, and wildcard exemptions before filing.

Homestead Exemption

Protects equity in your primary residence. Amounts vary by state. Check Wyoming homestead exemption amounts.

Vehicle Exemption

Protects equity in your car. Most states protect $2,000 to $7,500. See vehicle exemption details.

Means Test Calculator
Discharge Screener

Filing Bankruptcy in Wyoming

Districts

Wyoming has a single federal bankruptcy district. All cases are filed in this one court, which simplifies the process.

Filing Costs

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338. Chapter 13: $313. Fee waivers are available for filers below 150% of the poverty line. Installment payments available for all filers. Full cost breakdown.

Before You File

Complete credit counseling from an approved provider (required within 180 days before filing).

Determine your chapter -- Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 comparison.

Gather income documentation for the last 6 full calendar months.

List all debts, assets, income, and expenses for the bankruptcy schedules.

Research Wyoming exemptions to understand what property you can keep.

After You File

The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and lawsuits.

Attend the 341 meeting of creditors (usually 30-45 days after filing).

Complete a debtor education course (required before discharge).

Receive your discharge (Chapter 7: ~60-90 days after 341 meeting; Chapter 13: after completing 3-5 year plan).

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Wyoming?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees in Wyoming typically range from $1,000 to $3,500 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $5,000 for Chapter 13. You can also file without an attorney (pro se) and request to pay the filing fee in installments.

What is the income limit for Chapter 7 in Wyoming?

The means test compares your household income to Wyoming's median. For a single person, the current threshold is approximately $71,571 per year. For a family of four, it is approximately $126,681. If your income is below the median for your household size, you pass Part 1 of the means test.

What property can I keep in Wyoming bankruptcy?

Wyoming requires filers to use state exemptions only. Key exemptions to research include the homestead exemption (how much home equity you can protect), vehicle exemption, personal property exemption, and any wildcard exemption. Check Wyoming statutes for current amounts.

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit in Wyoming?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, many people begin rebuilding credit within 1-2 years of discharge and can qualify for a mortgage within 2-4 years.

Can I file bankruptcy again in Wyoming?

Yes, but there are waiting periods between discharges. Chapter 7 to Chapter 7: 8 years. Chapter 7 to Chapter 13: 4 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 13: 2 years. Chapter 13 to Chapter 7: 6 years (unless you paid 100% or 70%+ with good faith). Use the free discharge eligibility screener to check your dates.

What debts cannot be discharged in bankruptcy?

Most debts are dischargeable, but exceptions include most student loans (unless you prove undue hardship), child support and alimony, recent tax debts (generally last 3 years), debts from fraud or willful injury, DUI-related debts, and criminal fines. See nondischargeable.org for the full list.

Bankruptcy in Nearby States

Montana · South Dakota · Nebraska · Colorado

## Vehicle Exemptions in Bankruptcy -- How Much Car Equity Is Protected
Source: https://bankruptcyexemptionsbystate.com/vehicle-exemptions.html

bankruptcyexemptionsbystate.com
☰

Home
Federal
Homestead
Vehicle
Wildcard
Retirement
Personal Property
Opt-Out States
FAQ

Vehicle Exemptions in Bankruptcy

Most states protect between $2,500 and $7,500 in vehicle equity. Here is how vehicle exemptions work and how to protect your car.

How vehicle exemptions work

The vehicle exemption protects your equity in a motor vehicle. Equity is the difference between the car's fair market value and what you owe on it. If you have a car loan and owe more than the car is worth, you have zero equity -- and the vehicle exemption is not even needed.

The federal vehicle exemption under 11 U.S.C. § 522(d)(2) is $4,450. State exemptions vary from as little as $1,000 to over $10,000, and some states have no specific vehicle exemption at all (but may offer a generous wildcard).

Selected state vehicle exemptions

StateVehicle Exemption

Federal$4,450

California (System 1)$7,500

Texas1 vehicle per licensed household member

Florida$1,000 (but can use $4,000 wildcard if not using homestead)

New York$4,825

Ohio$4,450

Missouri$3,000

Michigan$3,875 (or federal $4,450)

Illinois$2,400

Pennsylvania$0 (state law; use federal $4,450)

Multiple vehicles

Most state vehicle exemptions apply per debtor, not per vehicle. If you own two cars, you can typically only exempt one. The second vehicle would need to be covered by a wildcard exemption or other available exemption. In joint filings, each spouse can exempt one vehicle.

Texas is a notable exception -- each licensed household member can exempt one vehicle, regardless of value.

What if your equity exceeds the exemption?

If your car equity exceeds the exemption, the Chapter 7 trustee could theoretically sell the car. But in practice, the trustee considers the economics: selling a car involves transaction costs, and the trustee must pay you the exempt amount from the proceeds. If the non-exempt equity is only a few hundred dollars, most trustees abandon the vehicle.

You may also be able to use a wildcard exemption to cover the gap, or negotiate with the trustee to pay the non-exempt amount to keep the vehicle.

Most people keep their cars in bankruptcy. Between loan balances reducing equity, exemption amounts, and the practical economics of vehicle sales, trustees rarely take cars in consumer Chapter 7 cases.

## Wages Exemption in Bankruptcy -- Protecting Your Paycheck
Source: https://bankruptcyexemptionsbystate.com/wages-exemption-bankruptcy.html

bankruptcyexemptionsbystate.com
☰

Home
Federal Exemptions
Homestead
FAQ
By State

Wages Exemption in Bankruptcy -- Protecting Your Paycheck

How much of your wages are protected in bankruptcy? Federal and state wage exemptions, garnishment limits, and how exemptions apply to bank deposits.

Home › Wages Exemption in Bankruptcy -- Protecting Your Paycheck

Protecting Your Wages in Bankruptcy

Wage protection in bankruptcy depends on both federal and state exemptions:

Federal wage garnishment limit: Under the Consumer Credit Protection Act, only 25% of disposable earnings can be garnished (or the amount by which weekly earnings exceed 30 times the federal minimum wage, whichever is less).

Bankruptcy automatic stay: Filing bankruptcy immediately stops all wage garnishment under the automatic stay (Section 362).

State exemptions: Many states have additional wage exemptions that protect more of your paycheck. Some states (like Texas and South Carolina) have very strong wage protections.

Wages in bank accounts: Once wages are deposited into a bank account, they may lose their status as "wages" and become cash -- which has different exemption rules. Some states specifically protect deposited wages for a period of time.

## Wildcard Exemptions in Bankruptcy -- Protect Any Property
Source: https://bankruptcyexemptionsbystate.com/wildcard-exemptions.html

bankruptcyexemptionsbystate.com
☰

Home
Federal
Homestead
Vehicle
Wildcard
Retirement
Personal Property
Opt-Out States
FAQ

Wildcard Exemptions in Bankruptcy

Wildcard exemptions are flexible -- apply them to any property: cash, bank accounts, tax refunds, or anything else not covered by specific exemptions.

What is a wildcard exemption?

Unlike category-specific exemptions (homestead for your home, vehicle for your car), a wildcard exemption can be applied to any property. It gives you flexible protection for assets that do not fit neatly into other exemption categories -- like cash in a bank account, a tax refund, cryptocurrency, or valuable personal property.

The federal wildcard under 11 U.S.C. § 522(d)(5) provides $1,475 plus up to $13,950 of any unused portion of the homestead exemption. If you do not own a home (or do not need the full homestead exemption), the total wildcard can reach $15,425 per debtor -- or $30,850 for a married couple filing jointly.

Selected state wildcard exemptions

State/SchemeWildcard AmountNotes

Federal (§ 522(d)(5))$1,475 + up to $13,950 unused homesteadMax $15,425 per debtor

California (System 2)$33,650Available if not using System 1 homestead

Missouri$600Very limited

Ohio$1,325Can be applied to any property

New York$1,175State wildcard; federal wildcard also available

Michigan$1,475 + $13,950 unused homestead (federal)Can use federal exemptions

Georgia$1,200 per debtorPlus unused portion of $21,500 homestead

Indiana$10,250Generous standalone wildcard

Kentucky$1,000Federal exemptions also available

Strategic uses of the wildcard

Protect cash and bank accounts. Money in checking or savings accounts on the filing date is property of the estate. The wildcard can cover it.

Cover tax refunds. Expected tax refunds are assets. If you file in January, your upcoming refund may need wildcard protection.

Top up vehicle equity. If your car equity exceeds the vehicle exemption by $2,000, use $2,000 of wildcard to cover the gap.

Protect non-traditional assets. Cryptocurrency, digital assets, valuable domain names, or pending settlements can all be covered by the wildcard.

You cannot mix federal and state exemptions. Under § 522(b)(1), you must choose one system or the other. If your state allows federal exemptions and the federal wildcard is more generous, you may want to use the entire federal scheme -- even if the state has better exemptions in some categories.

## Is Your 401(k) Protected in Bankruptcy? (2026)
Source: https://bankruptcyexemptionsbystate.com/401k-protected-in-bankruptcy.html

Skip to main content

bankruptcyexemptionsbystate.com
☰

Home
Federal
Homestead
Vehicle
Retirement
Wildcard

401(k)

Is Your 401(k) Protected
in Bankruptcy?

Yes -- 401(k), 403(b), and most retirement accounts are protected without limit. Complete guide to retirement protection.

The Short Answer: Yes

Your 401(k) is fully protected in bankruptcy -- both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13. The Employee Retirement Income Security Act (ERISA) provides unlimited federal protection for most employer-sponsored retirement plans. No bankruptcy trustee can touch your 401(k).

This protection applies regardless of the balance. Whether your 401(k) has $5,000 or $5 million, it is fully exempt.

What's Protected Without Limit

401(k) plans

403(b) plans

457 plans

Defined benefit pension plans

Profit-sharing plans

Employee stock ownership plans (ESOPs)

Keogh plans

Most other ERISA-qualified plans

The key: these are plans governed by ERISA. As long as your employer's plan meets ERISA requirements (almost all do), the protection is unlimited.

IRAs: Protected With a Cap

Traditional IRAs, Roth IRAs, and SEP-IRAs are protected up to a combined limit of approximately $1,512,350 (adjusted for inflation). Amounts above this cap could theoretically be taken by the trustee, though this rarely happens.

Rollover IRAs (funds rolled over from an employer plan into an IRA) may retain the unlimited ERISA protection of the original plan, depending on your jurisdiction.

Critical Warning: Do NOT Withdraw

Never withdraw from retirement accounts to pay debts before filing bankruptcy. Your retirement funds are protected in bankruptcy. Withdrawing them converts protected retirement assets into non-exempt cash or payments to creditors you could have discharged. You lose the money AND owe taxes and penalties on the withdrawal.

This is one of the most common and costly pre-bankruptcy mistakes. If a debt collector or financial advisor suggests using retirement funds to pay debts, consult a bankruptcy attorney first.

What About Retirement Income?

Retirement income you've already received (pension payments, 401(k) distributions deposited in your bank account) loses its ERISA protection. Once the money is in a regular bank account, it is protected only by your state's cash or bank account exemption -- which may be limited.

Strategy: keep retirement account funds in the retirement account until you need them. Don't accumulate large balances in checking accounts from retirement distributions.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is my 401(k) safe in Chapter 7?

Yes. 401(k) plans are protected without limit under ERISA in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13. The trustee cannot touch your 401(k) regardless of the balance.

Is my IRA protected in bankruptcy?

Yes, up to approximately $1.5 million (adjusted for inflation). This limit covers your combined Traditional and Roth IRA balances. Rollover IRAs from employer plans may have unlimited protection.

Should I use my 401(k) to pay debts before filing?

Absolutely not. Your 401(k) is protected in bankruptcy -- the debts are not. Withdrawing to pay debts costs you the money, plus taxes, plus early withdrawal penalties, for debts that could have been discharged.

What about Social Security in bankruptcy?

Social Security benefits are fully protected in bankruptcy. They are also excluded from the means test, making Chapter 7 qualification easier for retirees.

Related Topics on This Site

Retirement Exemptions

Federal Exemptions

Personal Property

## Wage and Income Exemptions in Bankruptcy (2026)
Source: https://bankruptcyexemptionsbystate.com/wage-exemptions.html

Skip to main content

bankruptcyexemptionsbystate.com
☰

Home
Federal
Homestead
Vehicle
Retirement
Wildcard

Wages

Wage and Income
Exemptions in Bankruptcy

How much of your income is protected from creditors and the trustee. State and federal protections compared.

Wage Protection in Bankruptcy

Your wages earn special protection in bankruptcy through a combination of federal and state exemption laws. The automatic stay immediately stops wage garnishments, and exemptions protect your earnings from the trustee.

Federal Garnishment Limits

Federal law (15 U.S.C. section 1673) limits garnishment to 25% of disposable earnings or the amount by which weekly earnings exceed 30 times the federal minimum wage, whichever is less. Many states provide even greater protection.

State Wage Exemptions

States that protect more than the federal minimum include: Texas and South Carolina (100% of wages exempt from garnishment for most debts), Pennsylvania (100% exempt except for specific debts), North Carolina (100% for the first $500/month with percentage above).

Check your state's specific wage exemption, as protections vary significantly.

Protecting Wages in Your Bank Account

Once wages are deposited in your bank account, they may lose their wage exemption protection and become subject to your state's general bank account exemption instead. Some states have "tracing" rules that protect deposited wages for a period (commonly 30-60 days).

Strategy: file when your bank balance is low, or maintain a separate account for non-wage income to simplify tracing.

Frequently Asked Questions

Are my wages protected in bankruptcy?

Yes. The automatic stay stops garnishments immediately. Federal law limits garnishment to 25% of disposable earnings, and many states provide greater protection.

Can the trustee take my paycheck?

In Chapter 7, your post-filing income is generally not part of the bankruptcy estate -- your future paychecks are yours. In Chapter 13, your disposable income funds the plan.

What about wages already in my bank account?

Deposited wages may lose their wage exemption protection depending on state law. Some states allow tracing of wages in bank accounts for 30-60 days.

Does the automatic stay stop wage garnishment?

Yes, immediately upon filing. Your employer must stop garnishment as soon as they are notified of your bankruptcy filing.

Related Topics on This Site

Federal Exemptions

Personal Property

Homestead Exemptions

## Tools of the Trade Exemption in Bankruptcy (2026)
Source: https://bankruptcyexemptionsbystate.com/tools-of-trade.html

Skip to main content

bankruptcyexemptionsbystate.com
☰

Home
Federal
Homestead
Vehicle
Retirement
Wildcard

Tools

Tools of the Trade
Exemption

Protect the equipment, tools, and instruments you need to earn a living. Exemptions vary by state.

What Qualifies as Tools of the Trade

Tools of the trade exemptions protect equipment, instruments, and supplies you need for your occupation. This can include: hand tools and power tools for tradespeople, computers and software for office workers, musical instruments for musicians, cameras for photographers, farm equipment for farmers, and professional libraries for lawyers.

The definition is interpreted broadly in most jurisdictions -- anything reasonably necessary for your employment qualifies.

Federal Exemption

The federal tools of the trade exemption (11 U.S.C. section 522(d)(6)) protects up to $2,800 in tools, implements, and professional books. This amount is relatively low, but can be supplemented with the wildcard exemption.

State Exemptions

State exemptions vary dramatically. Some highlights: Texas: unlimited tools of the trade (one of the most generous). Kansas: up to $7,500. California System 1: up to $9,525. Missouri: up to $3,000. Some states have no specific tools exemption.

If your state's exemption is insufficient, check whether you can use the federal exemptions instead (available in states that allow the choice).

Self-Employed Filers

Self-employed filers often have more at stake: commercial equipment, inventory, and specialized tools. The tools of the trade exemption can be critical for sole proprietors who need their equipment to earn a living.

In Chapter 7, protecting business equipment through exemptions lets you continue working. In Chapter 13, all assets are kept regardless, but the value of non-exempt assets increases your plan payments.

Frequently Asked Questions

What counts as tools of the trade?

Any equipment, instruments, or supplies reasonably necessary for your occupation. This includes hand tools, computers, professional books, musical instruments, farm equipment, and more.

How much can I exempt?

Federal: $2,800. State amounts vary from $0 to unlimited (Texas). Check your state's specific exemption or consult an attorney.

Can I protect a work vehicle?

Work vehicles may be covered under the tools of the trade exemption if used primarily for work. They may also be covered under the vehicle exemption. In some cases, both exemptions can apply.

What about my home office equipment?

Home office equipment used for your trade or profession qualifies. This includes computers, desks, printers, and software reasonably necessary for your work.

Related Topics on This Site

Federal Exemptions

Vehicle Exemptions

Personal Property
